pathB.title: Grounded
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel grounded. Your system has finally accepted the support of the earth, allowing your structure to rest without effort.
pathB.blueprint: Passive Proprioceptive Loading. Your nervous system has stopped the constant micro-adjustments of 'standing guard,' allowing the weight of your skeleton to be fully managed by external surfaces.
pathB.physicalSensation: A strong awareness of the contact points between your body and the chair/floor; a low center of gravity; and a sense of being 'heavy' but safe.
pathB.metaphor: An anchor at the bottom of a calm bay. There is no tension in the chain, and the sand holds the weight perfectly.
pathB.theImpulse: To exhale deeply, to sink further into your seat, and to enjoy the feeling of 'not having to go anywhere'.
pathB.superpower: Structural Integrity. You are unshakeable. Because you aren't fighting gravity, you have the maximum capacity for internal repair and steady observation.
pathB.generalGoal: Deepening the yield. Transitioning from 'holding' to 'being held'.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside (Heaviness)
pathA.goal: The 'weight' feels a bit too internal or sleepy. Goal: Upward Expansion.

body.text: The Pelvic Breath. As you inhale, imagine the breath traveling down to your sit-bones; as you exhale, feel your spine grow 1cm taller without losing the heavy seat.
body.physioTip: Best for: Preventing 'collapse' into lethargy by maintaining a micro-engagement of the deep postural stabilizers (multifidus) while remaining grounded.

mind.text: The Internal Compass. Keep your eyes closed. Identify exactly where North is based on your current seated position. Feel your orientation in space.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Gently re-engaging spatial awareness to prevent the 'internalized' heaviness from becoming a total withdrawal from the environment.

social.text: The Steady Beacon. Imagine yourself as a lighthouse. You are fixed and heavy at the base, but your awareness (the light) is scanning the horizon with ease.
social.physioTip: Best for: Transitioning from 'internal rest' to 'social readiness' by using imagery that combines stability with outward-facing awareness.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside (Disconnection)
pathB.goal: You are 'hovering' over the support rather than sinking into it. Goal: The Deep Yield.

body.text: Passive Loading. Identify one body part (like your forearms or calves) and consciously 'give' its entire weight to the surface it's touching. Let the chair do 100% of the work.
body.physioTip: Best for: Reducing residual 'background' muscle tone (guarding) by proving to the brain that the environment is physically capable of supporting the load.

mind.text: Structural Solidarity. Find a vertical line (a wall or pillar). Look at it and think: 'That wall is holding the roof, and the floor is holding me. We are both supported.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visual structural cues to provide the 'logic' for emotional safety, facilitating a deeper parasympathetic settle.

social.text: The Shared Floor. Notice the person nearest to you (or a pet). Realize you are both being held up by the same earth. Feel the silent, solid connection beneath you.
social.physioTip: Best for: Creating a sense of 'common humanity' and social safety by anchoring to a shared physical reality (the ground).

pathB.warning: N/A

pathB.title: Frozen
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel frozen. Your system has detected a threat that feels inescapable, so it has crystallized your state to protect you from impact. You aren't 'stuck'; you are currently in a protective stasis.
pathB.blueprint: Biological crystallization. Your nervous system has 'iced over' to shield your core identity. This is a high-level defensive immobilization designed to wait out a storm.
pathB.physicalSensation: A rigid, icy stillness. You may feel cold in your hands and feet, a shallow 'locked' breath, and a heavy, immovable weight behind your eyes.
pathB.metaphor: An arctic landscape mid-winter. Everything is preserved in ice, motionless and silent, holding its breath until the spring.
pathB.theImpulse: To stay perfectly still, to stop time, and to avoid any movement that might 'break' the protective shell.
pathB.superpower: Preservation. You are currently a biological time-capsule. Your system is protecting your most precious internal resources from being touched by a harsh outside world.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are trapped in the internal ice. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Friction Warmth. Gently rub your hands together to create a tiny bit of friction-warmth and place them over your heart.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using localized thermal input to signal 'the thaw' to the heart-center.

mind.text: The Internal Flame. Visualize a single candle flame in your chest. As you exhale slowly, imagine the warmth spreading to your shoulders.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using focused imagery to gently increase interoceptive warmth without physical exertion.

social.text: The Lighthouse. Imagine the face of a person who makes you feel safe. Just see them there.
social.physioTip: Best for: Accessing safe attachment figures internally to lower the 'threat-detection' response.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels cold and unreachable. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Warmth Anchor. Wrap your hands around a warm cup or hold a soft fabric against your cheek.
body.physioTip: Best for: Shifting the sensory context from 'frozen/rigid' to 'warm/malleable' using external textures.

mind.text: Soft Scanning. Name one thing in the room that is physically 'soft' or 'round'.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Training the eyes to look for 'safety cues' (organic, non-sharp shapes) in the environment.

social.text: Safe Proximity. If safe, ask a person or pet to sit in the same room as you, without talking.
social.physioTip: Best for: Experiencing co-presence and 'holding space' without the demand of verbal output.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are trapped in the internal ice. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Edge Scan. Touch the edge of a hard object (table, phone, chair). Trace the cold, clear boundary of the object with your index finger.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'boundary data' to pull awareness out of the internal void and back into the physical room.

mind.text: Color Hunting. Find three items in the room that are NOT white or grey. Say their colors out loud.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'internal monochrome' of a frozen state by forcing the visual cortex to process high-contrast external data.

social.text: Safe Proximity. If a safe person or pet is nearby, simply look at their feet or their back. Notice their solid presence in the room with you.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'passive co-regulation' to tether your nervous system to a safe external living signal.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels cold and unreachable. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Core Thaw. Gently rub your palms together to create friction-warmth, then place them directly over your heart or solar plexus.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing a localized 'heat signal' to the chest to encourage the return of blood flow to the core.

mind.text: The Internal Flame. Visualize a single, steady candle flame at the very center of your chest. Feel its tiny radius of warmth.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Creating a safe, micro-focused internal anchor to help re-inhabit the body without feeling overwhelmed.

social.text: The Internal Lighthouse. Close your eyes and visualize the face of someone who makes you feel safe. Imagine them looking at you with a gentle smile.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'introjects' (internalized safety figures) to create a warm internal social climate.

pathB.pathB: Path A = Out / Path B = In

pathB.title: Shutdown
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel shut down. Numbness isn't a lack of feeling; it’s a high-level protective gear your brain wears to prevent a total system crash. You are not broken; your body is successfully executing an ancient survival protocol.
pathB.blueprint: The 'Vault' response. Your system has determined the environment is too costly to engage with. It has pulled the emergency brake to protect your vital organs.
pathB.physicalSensation: A heavy, leaden weight throughout the body. A sense of being 'switched off' or disconnected from the motor controls.
pathB.metaphor: A deep-sea creature retreating into a dark trench where the pressure is immense, but the world above cannot reach it.
pathB.theImpulse: To curl into a ball, pull the covers over your head, and completely disappear from view.
pathB.superpower: Absolute Containment. You are currently a fortress. Your system is prioritizing core survival by cutting off all external noise.
pathB.generalGoal: The 'Thaw'. A tiny pulse of presence.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel heavy and trapped inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Palate Press. Gently press your tongue against the roof of your mouth and feel the texture of your palate.
body.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating cranial nerves and internal awareness through low-effort oral proprioception.

mind.text: The Vocal Count. Count backwards from 10 out loud to engage your vocal cords and the room.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Activating the vocal apparatus to break the 'silent' shutdown state and re-occupy the space.

social.text: Passive Safety. Listen to a podcast with a familiar, comforting voice to cue biological safety.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using human prosody (voice tone) as a safe 'anchor' without the demand of a response.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel spaced out and detached from your body. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Thermal Palm-Press. Rub your hands together for 30 seconds to generate heat, then place them over your eyes.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using localized heat to soothe the optic nerve and pull awareness back into the physical self.

mind.text: Texture Scan. Notice the texture of one surface near you—the grain of wood, a weave of fabric, or a smooth wall.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the visual system in a low-intensity, rhythmic scan of the current environment.

social.text: Neutral Witness. Acknowledge a plant or chair as a neutral, safe presence in your space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing a sense of 'being seen' or 'being with' non-judgmental objects when human contact feels too loud.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel heavy and trapped in the 'Vault'. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The 180-Degree Horizon. Without moving your head, slowly let your eyes drift to the furthest left and furthest right points of the room.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'peripheral scan' to tell the brainstem the room is safe and open, gently nudging the system out of the 'hidden' shutdown state.

mind.text: The Vocal Pulse. Count backward from 10 out loud. Try to feel the vibration of your own voice in the air around you.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'internal silence' of shutdown by using the vocal cords to occupy physical space and signal presence.

social.text: The Audio Anchor. Turn on a podcast or music with a warm, melodic human voice (prosody). Focus on the 'rhythm' of the talk, not the content.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'passive social engagement'—receiving safety signals through vocal tones without the burden of interacting.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel spaced out and detached. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Palate Press. Press your tongue firmly against the roof of your mouth. Notice the exact texture and the pressure on your hard palate.
body.physioTip: Best for: Activating the trigeminal nerve and 'midline' receptors to pull a dissociated brain back into the physical structure of the head.

mind.text: Thermal Eye-Press. Rub your palms together to create heat, then cup them over your closed eyes. Feel the darkness and the warmth seep in.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using 'compression and heat' to provide a high-contrast internal signal that anchors the user back into their own skull.

social.text: Internal Witness. Acknowledge your own heartbeat or the weight of your own hands. Say silently: 'I am back inside this body.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-establishing 'self-as-anchor,' confirming that the physical body is a safe and occupied territory.

pathB.title: Numb
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel numb. Numbness is a biological anesthetic; your brain has turned down the volume on all input to save you from feeling too much at once.
pathB.blueprint: Sensory Dampening. The Dorsal Vagus is muting your feedback loops. This mechanism prevents emotional or physical overwhelm from reaching your processor.
pathB.physicalSensation: A 'cotton-wool' feeling in the head; a lack of tactile sensation in the skin; a feeling of being 'buffered' from the air.
pathB.metaphor: Walking through the world wearing a thick diving suit. You can see the world, but you can't feel the breeze.
pathB.theImpulse: To disconnect, to drift, and to remain in the 'gray' where nothing can touch you.
pathB.superpower: Impact Protection. You can exist in high-stress environments without absorbing the blow. You are in 'Armor' mode.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel lost in a gray void. Goal: Direct Sensation.

body.text: The Forearm Grip. Grip your forearms firmly and squeeze. Feel the actual pressure of your own hands.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing deep pressure input to pierce through the 'cotton-wool' sensation and locate the body's boundaries.

mind.text: Pressure Scan. Press your heels firmly into the ground and notice the resistance of the floor.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Creating a high-intensity pressure signal at the body's furthest point to re-establish the vertical axis.

social.text: Safe Ping. Text a safe person: 'Feeling a bit quiet.' No further explanation needed.
social.physioTip: Best for: Maintaining social visibility without the high metabolic cost of a full conversation.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like a silent movie. Goal: Sensory Tethers.

body.text: Rhythmic Tapping. Tap a rhythm on a hard surface and listen closely to the 'click' sound.
body.physioTip: Best for: Synchronizing hearing and touch to create a multi-channel anchor in the present moment.

mind.text: The Eye Trace. Pick an object and trace its outline with your eyes as if you were drawing it.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using active focal tracking to sharpen the 'low-resolution' perception typical of numbness.

social.text: Tactile Memory Trace. Look at a photo of a pet and try to recall exactly how their fur feels.
social.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating the sensory cortex by 'simulating' touch through safe emotional memory.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel lost in a gray void. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Temperature Shock. Find something cold (a glass of water, a stone, or a metal spoon). Hold it firmly and focus on the exact edge where the cold meets your skin.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using thermal contrast to pierce through the 'sensory buffer' and force the thalamus to process external data.

mind.text: The Eye Trace. Pick a complex object in the room. Trace its entire outline with your eyes as slowly as possible, as if drawing it in the air.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'vague scanning' to 'active tracking,' which re-engages the visual cortex with the external environment.

social.text: The Rhythmic Ping. Tap a clear rhythm on a hard surface (table/wall). Listen for the distinct 'click' it makes in the room.
social.physioTip: Best for: Synchronizing an internal action with an external sound to 'mark' your presence in the shared space.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like a silent movie. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Forearm Grip. Grip your own forearms firmly. Squeeze until you can feel the density of the muscle and the resistance of the bone underneath.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing high-intensity deep pressure (DTP) to provide the 'buffer' brain with a clear map of where the body begins.

mind.text: Heel-Loading. Press your heels firmly into the ground. Identify the exact spot on your feet where the most pressure is located.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing gravity and skeletal loading to 'pull' the dissociated awareness back down into the vertical axis of the body.

social.text: Tactile Memory Trace. Close your eyes. Recall exactly how a specific texture feels (e.g., a pet's fur or a favorite sweater). Try to feel it 'internally'.
social.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating the somatosensory cortex through 'emotive simulation' to re-sensitize the internal map of the self.

pathB.title: Disconnected
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel disconnected. When the environment feels unsafe or hostile, your brain protects you by 'unplugging' the data feed. You aren't losing your mind; you are using a biological shield.
pathB.blueprint: Dissociative buffering. Your nervous system is muting the connection between your 'self' and your surroundings to reduce the emotional impact of a perceived threat.
pathB.physicalSensation: A feeling of being 'behind glass' or looking at the world from far away. Your own voice may sound distant, and your limbs might feel like they don't quite belong to you.
pathB.metaphor: A radio station with a weak signal—there is a lot of static, and the 'real' broadcast feels like it's happening in another room.
pathB.theImpulse: To stare blankly, to drift into daydreams, and to avoid eye contact or any interaction that requires 'presence'.
pathB.superpower: The Great Buffer. You have the ability to survive overwhelming environments by distancing your core self from the immediate chaos.
pathB.generalGoal: Tactile grounding. Finding one physical tether.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel lost in an internal fog or a 'void'. Goal: Physical Presence.

body.text: The Chair Grip. Grip the edges of your chair or your own forearms as firmly as you can for 5 seconds, then release.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using isometric tension to provide high-intensity feedback to the brain about where the body is in space.

mind.text: The Identity Anchor. State your full name, the current day of the week, and your location out loud in a clear whisper.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Re-orienting the 'Thinking Brain' to time and place to interrupt the dissociative drift.

social.text: Hand Scan. Look at your own palm and trace the unique lines with one finger, acknowledging: 'This is my hand.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing 'Self-Affiliation' by visually and physically confirming the reality of your own body.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels fake or like a movie you aren't part of. Goal: Sensory Sharpness.

body.text: The Surface Press. Press your palms firmly against a cold wall or a hard table and notice the resistance.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using external resistance and temperature to provide a clear 'edge' to where the body ends and the world begins.

mind.text: The Texture Hunt. Find one object and name its texture: Is it rough, smooth, cold, or soft?
mind.physioTip: Best for: Training the brain to categorize detailed sensory data from the external world.

social.text: Tactile Memory Trace. Look at a photo of a pet and try to recall exactly how their fur feels under your hand.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using safe emotional memories to stimulate the sensory cortex and re-tether to relational safety.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel lost in an internal fog or a 'void'. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Surface Press. Press your palms firmly against a cold wall or a hard table. Notice the resistance of the object and the temperature of the surface.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using external resistance to provide a clear 'edge' where the body ends and the world begins, pulling awareness out of the internal void.

mind.text: The Identity Anchor. State your full name, the current day of the week, and your exact location out loud. Listen to the sound of your voice in the room.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'Acoustic Reflex' and vocalization to anchor the self to a specific time and physical coordinate in the environment.

social.text: The Texture Hunt. Pick one object in the room that you didn't notice before. Name its color and texture (e.g., 'That blue cushion is rough').
social.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to categorize and label external data, which interrupts the dissociative drift.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like a movie you aren't part of. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Humerus Squeeze. Cross your arms and grip your own upper arms (humerus) firmly. Squeeze for 5 seconds, feel the bone, and release.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing 'Deep Pressure Input' to the large muscles of the arms to remind the brain it is safely inside a physical container.

mind.text: The Hand Trace. Look at your own palm. Use one finger to slowly trace the lines of your hand. Say silently: 'This is my hand, I am here.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Establishing 'Self-Affiliation'—visually and physically confirming the reality of your own body to counter de-personalization.

social.text: Internal Safety Memory. Recall exactly how a specific, safe texture feels (e.g., a pet's fur or a heavy blanket). Try to feel it 'internally' on your skin.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'affective touch' memories to stimulate the insula and re-tether the self to internal biological reality.

pathB.title: Collapsed
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel collapsed. The demands became too heavy, and your body initiated a protective drop in energy to keep you safe. You are not weak; your biology is executing a damage-control protocol.
pathB.blueprint: Postural surrender. Your system has rapidly lost its sympathetic tone, causing your muscles to go slack to prevent a complete system fracture.
pathB.physicalSensation: A sinking feeling in the chest; slumped shoulders; a heavy chin. It feels like your bones have turned to lead and gravity has doubled.
pathB.metaphor: A tent that has lost its center pole in a storm; the fabric is still there, but the structure has given way to the weight of the rain.
pathB.theImpulse: To drop to the floor, curl into a ball, weep quietly, or surrender entirely to gravity.
pathB.superpower: Surrender. Your system wisely pulled the plug to prevent a break, allowing the storm to pass over you rather than through you.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel physically folded inward and heavy. Goal: Subtle Lift.

body.text: The Window Trace. Slowly uncurl your spine just an inch, lift your chin, and use your eyes to trace the outline of a doorway or window.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using the head-eye connection to gently re-engage the postural muscles along the spine.

mind.text: The Constant. Acknowledge one 'constant'—something that hasn't changed or moved (e.g., the floor, the wall).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a stable cognitive anchor when the internal sense of structure feels like it has given way.

social.text: Safe Witness. Mentally name one person or animal who has seen you at your worst and didn't leave.
social.physioTip: Best for: Recalling 'unconditional presence' to lower the shame often associated with collapse.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too bright, loud, or close. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Arm Squeeze. Squeeze your right hand down your left arm, from shoulder to wrist. Repeat on the other side.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using deep pressure to provide 'somatic containment,' helping the brain sense the physical limits of the body.

mind.text: The Breath Core. Notice the gentle expansion of your ribs against your back or chair as you inhale.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Feeling the internal pressure of the breath to provide a temporary 'internal pole' for the collapsed system.

social.text: The Maintenance Sign. Imagine a 'Closed for Maintenance' sign on your door. Give yourself permission to be 'offline'.
social.physioTip: Best for: Granting symbolic permission for metabolic recharge, reducing the 'drain' of social expectations.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel physically folded inward and heavy. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Window Trace. Slowly uncurl your spine just one inch and lift your chin. Use your eyes to trace the vertical outline of a doorway or window from top to bottom.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the 'head-eye' reflex to gently re-engage the deep spinal extensors without requiring high-energy output.

mind.text: The External Constant. Identify one large, unmoving object in the room (e.g., a heavy cupboard, a wall). Say silently: 'That is solid, that is there.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a stable external 'anchor' when the internal sense of structure feels like it has given way.

social.text: The Reliable Witness. Mentally name one person or animal who has remained present with you during difficult times. Visualize their face in the room with you.
social.physioTip: Best for: Recalling 'unconditional presence' to mitigate the shame-spiral often triggered by postural collapse.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too bright, loud, or close. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Arm Squeeze. Use your right hand to firmly squeeze down your left arm, from shoulder to wrist. Feel the bone underneath the muscle. Repeat on the other side.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'Deep Pressure Input' to create a somatic container, helping the brain perceive the physical limits of the self.

mind.text: The Breath Column. Inhale slowly and notice the internal pressure of the air against your ribs and back. Feel it acting as a temporary 'internal pole'.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the diaphragm to create intra-abdominal pressure, which provides a visceral sense of stability from the inside out.

social.text: Maintenance Permission. Imagine a 'Closed for Maintenance' sign on your door. Tell yourself: 'It is safe to be offline right now. I am recharging.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Granting symbolic permission for metabolic recharge, reducing the 'drain' caused by social-monitoring neurons.

pathB.title: Sinking
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel like you are sinking. When progress feels stalled, your body lets go of its 'upward' tension to save fuel for the long haul.
pathB.blueprint: Gravity surrender. Your sympathetic 'up' energy has dipped, allowing you to descend into a heavy, metabolic rest state.
pathB.physicalSensation: A dragging feeling in the pelvis and legs, as if your chair is made of quicksand. Your chest feels heavy and your breath feels deep and slow.
pathB.metaphor: An anchor slowly descending into deep water—away from the turbulent surface and into the stillness of the bottom.
pathB.theImpulse: To stop trying, to let your body go limp, and to slide down until you can't go any lower.
pathB.superpower: Finding the Floor. You cannot sink forever; eventually, you hit the bottom, and that becomes the solid ground you can push off from.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are descending into heavy internal depth. Goal: Micro-Expansion, Pull OUT.

body.text: The Heavy Sink. Let your weight drop fully into the chair for 3 breaths, then notice where you feel the most support
body.physioTip: Best for: Surrendering to the sink fully until the system registers that the floor/chair is successfully holding you.

mind.text: Buoyancy Imagery. Imagine one thing that naturally floats (a balloon, a cork, a cloud).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Priming the brain with 'upward' concepts to gently shift the metabolic tone away from descent.

social.text: The Lift. Think of one person who always lifts your mood just by being themselves.
social.physioTip: Best for: Accessing safe attachment memories to spark a tiny pulse of sympathetic 'up' energy.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel heavy from external pressure. Goal: Internal Support.

body.text: The Heel Press. Press your heels firmly into the ground and feel the floor 'pushing back' against you.
body.physioTip: Best for: Activating the posterior chain muscles to find 'the bottom' and establish a solid base for future movement.

mind.text: Line Finding. Find one horizontal line in the room (a shelf, a floorboard) and follow it with your eyes.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using stable environmental horizons to settle a dizzy or 'adrift' nervous system.

social.text: Weight. Ask a safe person for a firm hand-on-the-shoulder or use a weighted blanket to feel 'contained'.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing deep pressure input to the shoulders to signal that you are physically supported and 'held'.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are descending into heavy internal depth. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Horizon Finding. Find one long horizontal line in the room (a shelf, a floorboard). Follow it slowly with your eyes from left to right and back again.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using a stable environmental horizon to provide the brain with 'level' data, preventing the internal sensation of sinking further.

mind.text: Buoyancy Imagery. Visualize one thing that naturally floats (a balloon, a cork, a cloud). See it bobbing effortlessly on the surface of the water.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Priming the brain with 'upward' concepts to gently shift the metabolic tone away from vertical descent.

social.text: The External Lift. Think of one specific person who 'elevates' the energy in a room just by walking in. Visualize their face and their posture.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'social mirroring' neurons to spark a tiny pulse of sympathetic 'up' energy from a safe external source.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel heavy or adrift from external pressure. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Heel-Strike Resistance. Press your heels firmly into the ground. Feel the floor 'pushing back' against your skeleton. Acknowledge the solid resistance.
body.physioTip: Best for: Activating the posterior chain and mechanoreceptors in the feet to establish 'the bottom' and a solid base for the self.

mind.text: Finding the Floor. Let your weight drop fully into the chair for 3 breaths. Mentally locate the exact points where your body meets the support.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the system to register that the descent has stopped because the floor/chair is successfully holding the load.

social.text: Shoulder Loading. If safe, place your own hands firmly on your shoulders and push down slightly. Feel the 'containment' and the weight.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing deep pressure input to the 'top' of the system to signal that you are physically held and grounded from within.

pathB.title: Exhausted
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel exhausted. Your system is ringing the 'empty' bell to prevent you from doing permanent damage to your internal engine.
pathB.blueprint: Metabolic bankruptcy. You have spent more than you have earned, and your system is enforcing a mandatory 'spending freeze'.
pathB.physicalSensation: A burning or aching in the muscles; heavy, dry eyes; a 'fuzzy' or blurred perimeter to your thoughts.
pathB.metaphor: A runner who has crossed the finish line and simply dropped to the grass. The race is over; the recovery has begun.
pathB.theImpulse: To close your eyes mid-sentence, to stop all decision-making, and to sleep for an indefinite amount of time.
pathB.superpower: The Biological Truth. Exhaustion is your body's most honest boundary. It is the wisdom that knows when 'more' is no longer possible.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the internal ache of depletion. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Soft Drop. Scan your jaw and belly. If they are tight, give them permission to 'drop' for 5 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Releasing 'bracing' patterns in the core and TMJ to signal the body that the effort phase is over.

mind.text: Zero-Tasking. Give yourself permission to think about 'absolutely nothing' for one minute.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Halting the metabolic cost of cognitive processing and executive decision-making.

social.text: The Privacy Shield. Imagine a 'Do Not Disturb' sign hanging on your chest.
social.physioTip: Best for: Reducing the 'social scanning' reflex that drains energy when in the presence of others.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too bright and fast for your tired eyes. Goal: Dimming the Input. Pull IN

body.text: Eye Palming. Cup your palms over your eyes to create total darkness for 30 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Giving the optic nerve a total break, which immediately lowers sympathetic arousal in the brain.

mind.text: The Weighted Blanket. Notice the weight of your clothes or a blanket on your body and let it feel 5% heavier.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using gravity as a passive 'grounding' force to settle a vibrating, tired nervous system.

social.text: The Out-of-Office. Text a safe person: 'Rest mode on. Offline for a while.' No reply needed.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using active communication to protect your energy, removing the 'guilt' of non-responsiveness.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the internal ache of depletion. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Soft Release. Scan your jaw (TMJ) and your lower belly. If you find tightness, give those specific spots permission to 'drop' for 10 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Halting 'covert bracing' patterns that consume metabolic energy even when the system is supposedly resting.

mind.text: The Privacy Shield. Imagine a clear, semi-permeable 'Do Not Disturb' bubble around your chest and head. It keeps out all noise and expectations.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Reducing the 'social monitoring' cost in the prefrontal cortex, allowing the 'Self' to occupy space without being 'available'.

social.text: The Out-of-Office Ping. Text a safe person: 'Rest mode on. Offline for a while.' Acknowledge your boundary out loud to the room.
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing the 'Spending Freeze' to remove the background stress of social guilt or perceived demands.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too bright or fast for your tired eyes. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Eye Palming. Cup your palms over your closed eyes to create 100% darkness for 30 seconds. Feel the warmth of your hands on your face.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing a total blackout for the optic nerve to immediately down-regulate the sympathetic system and pull awareness back to the midline.

mind.text: Weighted Gravity. Focus on the weight of your clothes or a blanket on your shoulders. Mentally give 100% of your weight to the chair.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using 'passive grounding' to pull a spaced-out, tired brain back into the physical structure of the body.

social.text: Zero-Tasking Presence. Close your eyes. Notice only the internal rhythm of your heartbeat. Say silently: 'I am back inside, and I am resting.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-establishing an internal relationship of 'care' with the tired self, moving from 'doing' to 'inhabiting'.

pathB.title: Slumped
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel slumped. Your system has stopped 'holding' you upright against a world that feels heavy and discouraging.
pathB.blueprint: Postural deflation. The sympathetic 'tone' that keeps our spine erect has been turned down by the dorsal vagus, allowing gravity to take the lead.
pathB.physicalSensation: Curved shoulders, a drooping head, and a heavy pelvis. It feels like your muscles have lost their 'spring' and are just hanging off your bones.
pathB.metaphor: A sunflower at the end of a long, rain-heavy day—bowing its head because the weight of the water is more than it can carry.
pathB.theImpulse: To slide down in your chair, to rest your head in your hands, and to sigh deeply while looking at the floor.
pathB.superpower: Yielding to Gravity. You are resting the very muscles that usually have to work the hardest. You are letting the earth hold you for a moment.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel physically and emotionally folded in. Goal: Subtle Expansion.

body.text: The Palm Flip. Simply turn your palms upward to face the ceiling. Notice how this slightly opens your shoulders.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using the kinetic chain of the arm to naturally externally rotate the humerus and open the chest without muscular strain.

mind.text: The Crown String. Imagine a string attached to the very top of your head, gently pulling you upward by just one millimeter.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using ideokinesis to re-engage deep postural stabilizers along the spine without triggering a 'pushing' response.

social.text: The Shoulder Support. Visualize a friend giving you a gentle, supportive hand between your shoulder blades.
social.physioTip: Best for: Accessing safe tactile memory to provide a symbolic 'pillar' of support for the collapsed upper back.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like it's pressing down on your back. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Horizon Lift. Slowly lift your gaze from the floor to the furthest point you can see in the room or out a window.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using the eyes to lead the cervical spine into extension, signaling the brain to shift from 'threat-hiding' to 'horizon-scanning'.

mind.text: High-Point Scan. Identify three things in the room that are physically 'high up' (a lamp, a shelf, a picture).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'downward gaze' habit of morose states by forcing the eyes to move vertically.

social.text: The Rhythmic Tap. Listen to a song with a steady, uplifting beat and try to tap your finger to it.
social.physioTip: Best for: Entraining the nervous system to an external rhythm to pull the motor system out of a 'stalled' state.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel physically and emotionally folded in. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Horizon Lift. Slowly lift your gaze from the floor to the furthest point you can see in the room or out a window. Let your chin follow your eyes.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using the vestibulo-ocular reflex to gently lead the cervical spine into extension, signaling the brain to shift from 'internal hiding' to 'environmental scanning'.

mind.text: High-Point Scan. Identify three objects in the room that are physically 'high up' (a ceiling lamp, a high shelf, the top corner of a door).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'downward gaze' habit of morose states by forcing the oculomotor muscles to work in the vertical plane, which triggers postural alertness.

social.text: External Rhythmic Tap. Find a steady external sound (a clock, a distant bird, or a slow song). Tap your index finger to that rhythm, acknowledging the world's pace.
social.physioTip: Best for: Entraining the motor system to an external rhythm to pull the nervous system out of a 'stalled' internal loop.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like it is pressing down on you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Palm Flip. While seated, simply turn your palms upward to face the ceiling. Notice how your humerus rotates and your chest widens internally.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the kinetic chain of the arm to facilitate external rotation of the shoulders and open the thoracic cage without significant muscular effort.

mind.text: The Crown String. Imagine a thin, golden string attached to the very top of your head, gently lifting your skull by just one millimeter from the inside.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using ideokinesis (mental imagery of movement) to re-activate the deep postural stabilizers (multifidus) along the spine without triggering defensive tension.

social.text: The Internal Pillar. Visualize a safe, supportive presence (a friend or pet) standing right behind you, placing a gentle, warm hand between your shoulder blades.
social.physioTip: Best for: Accessing safe attachment memories to provide a symbolic 'internal pillar' of support for the upper back, reducing the feeling of being unprotected.

pathB.title: Retreating
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel like retreating. Your system is identifying a lack of safety and is proactively pulling back to prevent further exposure. You are not being 'difficult'; you are being protective.
pathB.blueprint: Proactive withdrawal. A mild dorsal vagal shift where the body prepares to shut the gates before a full drain occurs.
pathB.physicalSensation: A subtle pulling back in the chest, shoulders turning away from the center, and a desire to look at the exit.
pathB.metaphor: The tide pulling away from the shore, leaving the sand exposed and quiet.
pathB.theImpulse: To leave the room, stop the conversation, or physically turn your back to the environment.
pathB.superpower: Boundaried Wisdom. You are noticing a lack of safety early, allowing you to choose your exit before your battery hits zero.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the urge to hide within yourself. Goal: Gently pull OUT.

body.text: The Fetal Lock. Cross your arms and tuck your hands into your armpits. Feel the containment of your own frame.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing 'somatic shielding' and deep pressure to the core to lower the threat-response while remaining upright.

mind.text: The Invisible Wall. Imagine a clear, solid glass wall between you and whatever feels 'too much'.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Creating a symbolic cognitive boundary to reduce the emotional 'splash' from the environment.

social.text: The 30-Second Pause. Give yourself permission to not respond to the last thing said for 30 seconds.
social.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'reflexive compliance' of social interaction to reclaim a sense of agency and safety.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels repelling. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Neutral Gaze. Find one object in the room that is 'neutral'—neither good nor bad—and stare at it for 10 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Resting the amygdala by focusing on an emotionally 'empty' object, allowing the visual system to settle.

mind.text: The Leg Count. Count the number of legs on the furniture in your immediate view.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the 'task-positive network' in the brain to interrupt the emotional loop of disgust or retreat.

social.text: The Buffer Step. Step back physically by one foot. Notice if that tiny bit of extra space feels better.
social.physioTip: Best for: Adjusting the personal-space perimeter to provide the brain with an immediate 'spatial safety' cue.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the urge to hide within yourself. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Neutral Gaze. Find one object in the room that is 'neutral' (e.g., a door handle, a plain chair) and look at it steadily for 15 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Pulling awareness out of the internal 'disgust' loop by tethering the visual system to an emotionally empty object.

mind.text: The Furniture Count. Count the number of 'legs' on the furniture currently in your view. Focus on the hard, physical reality of the room.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the 'task-positive network' to anchor the brain in the external environment, reducing the urge to retreat internally.

social.text: The Buffer Step. If you are standing or sitting near others, physically shift back by one foot. Notice the feeling of reclaiming your own space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using movement to provide the brainstem with a clear 'spatial safety' cue, proving you are not trapped.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels abrasive or repelling. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Fetal Lock. Cross your arms and tuck your hands firmly into your opposite armpits. Feel the heat and the solid containment of your own frame.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing deep pressure and thermal input to the chest and axilla to signal internal safety, pulling awareness back into the physical container.

mind.text: The Invisible Wall. Imagine a clear, solid glass wall or a protective bubble between you and the environment. See it as an impenetrable boundary.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Creating a symbolic cognitive 'filter' that allows you to remain physically present while mentally inhabiting a private, safe space.

social.text: The 30-Second Pause. In any interaction, give yourself permission to wait 30 seconds before responding. Feel the 'pause' as an internal room you live in.
social.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the metabolic drain of 'social masking' by establishing an internal sanctuary of time and silence.

pathB.pathB: You are the authority over your own time.

pathB.title: Sagging
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel sagging. The internal pressure that holds you upright has dipped slightly, inviting you to lower your metabolic output.
pathB.blueprint: Mild postural release. Your sympathetic tone is fluctuating, allowing gravity to win the battle for your shoulders and chin.
pathB.physicalSensation: Heavy eyelids, a slight drooping of the mouth, and a feeling like your clothes are heavier than usual.
pathB.metaphor: A sail that has lost just enough wind to start fluttering and drooping, but the boat is still moving slowly.
pathB.theImpulse: To lean your head on your hand, to slump in your chair, and to speak in a quieter, flatter tone.
pathB.superpower: Micro-Rest. You are catching small pockets of recovery in the middle of the day, preventing a deeper collapse later.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the 'glum' weight internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Posterior Expansion. Inhale and imagine the air filling the space between your shoulder blades, lifting them slightly.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using breath volume to gently expand the upper thoracic spine and counteract the forward 'sag' of the shoulders.

mind.text: The Gentle Question. Ask yourself: 'What is one tiny thing that would feel slightly better right now?'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating mild agency and dopamine by identifying a low-effort improvement to the current state.

social.text: The Virtual Nod. Visualize a safe person giving you a quick, encouraging nod.
social.physioTip: Best for: Accessing brief, positive relational cues to trigger a micro-spark of social engagement energy.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world looks a bit gray and heavy. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Ceiling Scan. Lift your chin just enough to see where the wall meets the ceiling.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using a vertical gaze shift to reset the vestibular system and invite a minor lift in cervical extension.

mind.text: Saturation Search. Find one object that has a bright color and notice how the light hits it.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from a 'gray' visual bias to a higher-contrast focus, which can increase alertness in the brainstem.

social.text: The Low-Stakes Ping. If with someone, ask a very simple, low-stakes question like 'What's the weather like out there?'
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-entering the social engagement system through 'phatic' communication that requires zero emotional depth.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the 'glum' weight internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Ceiling Scan. Lift your chin just enough to see where the wall meets the ceiling in the corner of the room. Notice the angle.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using an upward vertical gaze to reset the vestibular system and provide a mechanical 'lift' cue to the cervical extensors.

mind.text: Saturation Search. Identify one object in the room with a bright or saturated color. Notice how the light defines its shape.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from the 'monochrome' bias of a low-energy state to high-contrast external data, which increases brainstem alertness.

social.text: The Phatic Ping. If someone is nearby, ask a neutral, 'boring' question (e.g., 'Do you know what time it is?'). If alone, text a friend a simple sun emoji ☀️.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using low-stakes social contact to gently nudge the ventral vagal system back into an 'active' state without emotional drain.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world looks a bit gray or heavy. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Posterior Expansion. Inhale and imagine the air filling the back of your lungs, specifically the space between your shoulder blades.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using internal breath volume to provide a 'pneumatic' lift to the upper thoracic spine, counteracting the forward sag.

mind.text: The Gentle Question. Ask your body silently: 'What is one tiny physical adjustment (a pillow, a glass of water) that would feel 1% better right now?'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating internal agency and a micro-dopamine spark by identifying a high-reward, low-effort improvement to the self.

social.text: The Virtual Support. Visualize a safe person giving you a quick, encouraging nod or a gentle hand on your shoulder. Feel the warmth of that idea.
social.physioTip: Best for: Accessing safe attachment imagery to provide an internal 'pillar' of support, signaling the system that it is safe to re-engage.

pathB.title: Dulled
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel dulled. Disappointment acts like a dampener on the senses, protecting you from the 'sharpness' of a recent letdown.
pathB.blueprint: Sensory muting. Your nervous system is slightly desensitizing your inputs to help you process a frustration without it feeling overwhelming.
pathB.physicalSensation: A 'fuzzy' or muffled quality to sounds and colors. A feeling of being slightly behind a thin veil.
pathB.metaphor: Looking through a window that has a layer of dust on it—the view is there, but the edges are soft and the colors are dim.
pathB.theImpulse: To stop looking for the 'good' in things and to settle for a neutral, muted experience.
pathB.superpower: Disappointment Buffer. You are giving your heart time to adjust to a change in plans by not feeling the full 'sting' all at once.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel internal 'static' or dullness. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Cutaneous Ping. Gently pinch the skin on the back of your hand. Notice the sharp, clear sensation of the touch.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using high-contrast tactile input to 'pierce' through sensory muting and re-alert the somatosensory cortex.

mind.text: Color Matching. Name three specific things you can see that are exactly the same color.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the visual system to engage in a specific search-and-sort task, which increases cognitive 'resolution'.

social.text: The Verbal Seal. Whisper one word that describes your disappointment, then take a slow breath.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the vibration of the voice to externalize and 'contain' an emotion without the demand of a full conversation.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels uninteresting or flat. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Thermal Contrast. Touch something cold (metal) and something warm (fabric) and notice the difference.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing temperature receptors to provide strong, distinct signals to the brain that interrupt the 'flat' feeling.

mind.text: The Long Word Hunt. Read the longest word you can find in the room.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the language-processing centers of the brain to shift focus from emotional 'fuzziness' to concrete data.

social.text: Texture Observation. Notice the texture of the clothing of someone nearby (e.g., knit, smooth, denim).
social.physioTip: Best for: Engaging in 'passive social curiosity' to stay tethered to the social world without requiring active output.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside

body.text: The Long Word Hunt. Scan the room for the longest word you can find on a book, sign, or screen. Read it slowly to yourself.
body.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the prefrontal cortex in a high-focus visual task to break the emotional 'fuzziness' and pull awareness into the room.

mind.text: Color Matching. Name three specific, separate objects in the room that are the exact same color. Focus on the shade.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the visual processing system to increase its contrast-detection, alerting the brain to external detail.

social.text: The Verbal Seal. Whisper one word that describes your current disappointment. Listen to the sound of your own voice in the air.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the vibration of the vocal cords to 'pierce' the internal silence and mark your physical presence in the environment.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside

body.text: The Cutaneous Ping. Firmly pinch the skin on the back of your hand or forearm. Focus entirely on the sharp point of pressure.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing high-contrast, localized tactile input to the somatosensory cortex, forcing the brain back into the 'container' of the skin.

mind.text: Thermal Contrast. Find something cold (metal) and something warm (your own neck). Alternate touching them and notice the distinct difference.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing temperature receptors to provide strong, distinct signals that interrupt the 'flat' feeling of dissociation.

social.text: Passive Curiosity. Look at an object or person nearby and mentally identify one specific texture (e.g., 'that sweater is knit', 'that table is slick').
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-establishing a safe, low-demand relational tether to the physical world through sensory observation.

pathB.title: Sluggish
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel sluggish. Your system is moving slowly to carefully navigate a situation where you feel a bit guarded or uncertain.
pathB.blueprint: High-viscosity movement. Your system is increasing the 'resistance' in your motor circuits to ensure you don't move faster than your current sense of safety allows.
pathB.physicalSensation: Movement feels 'thick'. Your thoughts are moving at a slower tempo, and your reaction time feels slightly delayed.
pathB.metaphor: An engine running with oil that is a bit too thick; it works, but every gear shift takes a bit more effort.
pathB.theImpulse: To take the easiest path, to procrastinate, and to move in 'slow motion'.
pathB.superpower: Deliberate Pace. By moving slowly, you are less likely to make a mistake or miss a subtle cue in your environment.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel 'slow' and heavy internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Bilateral Tap. Tap your fingers on your lap, alternating left and right, to a steady rhythm.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rhythmic bilateral stimulation to synchronize the brain hemispheres and reduce motor 'viscosity'.

mind.text: The Counted Breath. Count your breaths for 5 cycles: inhale (1), exhale (2), until you reach 10.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a simple, low-load cognitive track to follow, reducing the effort of internal decision-making.

social.text: The  Ping. Text a friend a 'slow' animal emoji to acknowledge your current tempo.
social.physioTip: Best for: Honoring your current metabolic speed in a social context, which removes the 'speed-up' pressure.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like it's moving too fast. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Smooth Pursuit. Follow a moving object with your eyes (a clock hand, a bird, a car) for 15 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Engaging 'smooth pursuit' eye movements to stabilize the visual field and calm the brainstem.

mind.text: Process Description. Silently describe one simple physical process happening in the room (e.g., 'The fan is spinning').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Anchoring the 'Thinking Brain' to objective reality to prevent it from falling behind the external pace.

social.text: The Buffer Request. Ask someone to repeat the last thing they said, allowing your brain time to fully 'process' the data.
social.physioTip: Best for: Creating a social 'buffer' that allows for neuro-synchronization when processing speeds are lowered.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel 'slow' and heavy internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Smooth Pursuit. Find a slowly moving object in the room (a clock hand, a swaying plant, a distant car). Follow it with your eyes for 15 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using external motor-tracking to 'drag' the brain out of its internal slow-tempo loop and re-engage the brainstem with the outside world.

mind.text: Process Description. Identify one simple physical process happening in the room (e.g., 'the fan is spinning', 'the light is reflecting'). Name it silently.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the 'Thinking Brain' to synchronize with external objective reality, breaking the internal 'viscosity' of thought.

social.text: The Tempo Ping. Text a friend a 'slow' animal emoji (🐢, 🦥). Acknowledging your pace to the world removes the internal pressure to 'speed up'.
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing your metabolic state to reduce social anxiety, which often adds a 'bracing' tax to an already sluggish system.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too bright or fast. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Bilateral Tap. Sit firmly. Tap your fingers on your lap, alternating left and right, to a steady, slow rhythm. Focus on the 'touch' on each leg.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using bilateral alternating stimulation to pull a 'spaced out' brain back into its midline and coordinate the hemispheres.

mind.text: The Counted Breath. Inhale (1), Exhale (2), until you reach 10. If you lose count, start over at 1. Keep your focus inside the ribs.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a closed-loop internal track for the brain to follow, anchoring the self back into the biological center.

social.text: The Buffer Request. If in conversation, ask: 'Could you say that again? I want to make sure I'm taking it all in.' Own your processing speed.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using active communication to create a 'social buffer,' allowing your internal speed to catch up with external data.

pathB.title: Foggy
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel foggy. Apathy is a way of protecting yourself from 'caring' too much when you feel vulnerable or over-exposed.
pathB.blueprint: Cognitive clouding. A mild dissociative state where the brain reduces the 'resolution' of your thoughts to prevent emotional overwhelm.
pathB.physicalSensation: A 'cloud' behind the forehead. Difficulty finding specific words and a sense of being 'spaced out' while still awake.
pathB.metaphor: Driving through a light mist; you can see the road, but the signs are hard to read until you are right on top of them.
pathB.theImpulse: To shrug your shoulders, to say 'I don't know' or 'I don't care', and to avoid complex tasks.
pathB.superpower: Mental Buffer. The fog acts like a soft cushion, preventing sharp thoughts or criticisms from landing too hard while you are feeling vulnerable.
pathB.generalGoal: Clearing the windshield. One clear point.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel 'cloudy' and disconnected from your goals. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Clearing Breath. Take a deep breath and exhale with a loud 'Ha!' sound. Feel the air leave your lungs.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using a forced exhale and vocalization to stimulate the diaphragm and break through respiratory 'stagnation'.

mind.text: The One-Task Anchor. Identify the single most simple thing you need to do next (e.g., 'Drink water').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Reducing cognitive load by collapsing complex 'foggy' thoughts into a single, actionable binary task.

social.text: The Literal Fact. State one true fact about your current environment out loud: 'The walls are white.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'Thinking Brain' to label objective reality, which helps pull consciousness out of a dissociative drift.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels blurry and uninteresting. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Near-Far Accommodation. Look at something very close (your palm) and then something very far away (out the window). Repeat 3 times.
body.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the ciliary muscles and the ocular system to force the brain to 'update' its visual resolution.

mind.text: Sharp Edge Trace. Find one object with a very sharp edge and trace it with your eyes.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using high-contrast visual data to 'sharpen' the boundaries between objects and the self.

social.text: The Color Note. Focus on the literal color of a safe person’s eyes or hair for 5 seconds.
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing a micro-connection through neutral, high-detail observation without the stress of interaction.

pathB.warning: THE THAW WARNING: Leaving the Blue Zone can feel scary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel 'cloudy' and lost in the internal mist. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Near-Far Accommodation. Hold your thumb close to your face. Focus on it for 3 seconds, then focus on an object at the furthest edge of the room. Repeat 3 times.
body.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the ciliary muscles to adjust, which sends an immediate 'update' signal to the brainstem to sharpen environmental awareness.

mind.text: The Literal Fact. State one objective truth about the room out loud: 'The floor is wood' or 'There is a lamp.' Listen to the sound of your voice.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'Thinking Brain' to label objective reality, which pulls consciousness out of a dissociative drift and into the room.

social.text: The Color Note. Focus on the literal, high-detail color of a safe person’s clothing or an object they own for 5 seconds.
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing an external micro-tether through high-detail observation, bypassing the 'fog' of apathy.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels blurry and you feel detached. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Clearing Breath. Take a deep inhale and exhale with a loud, sharp 'Ha!' sound. Feel the vibration in your chest and belly.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using forced exhalation to stimulate the diaphragm and the vagus nerve, signaling the brain to re-occupy the core of the body.

mind.text: The One-Task Anchor. Identify the single, most basic physical need you have right now (e.g., 'I need to adjust my seat' or 'I need a sip of water').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Reducing cognitive load by collapsing 'foggy' external data into a single, binary internal action.

social.text: Sharp Edge Trace. Find one object with a very sharp, clear edge. Trace that edge with your eyes while silently saying: 'I am here, and that is there.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using high-contrast boundaries to define the 'Self vs. Not-Self,' anchoring a spaced-out brain back into its own skin.

pathB.title: Exploding
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel like you are exploding. Your system has detected a critical boundary violation or threat and has flooded your engine with every drop of high-octane fuel to ensure your survival.
pathB.blueprint: Sympathetic Overload. The amygdala has hijacked the steering wheel, shutting down logic to provide maximum power to your limbs and lungs for an immediate fight-or-flight response.
pathB.physicalSensation: Extreme heat in the face and chest; a racing heart that feels like it might burst; jaw muscles clenched; and an overwhelming urge to move or strike.
pathB.metaphor: A solar flare or a steam boiler where the pressure gauge has hit the red line and the safety valve is screaming.
pathB.theImpulse: To strike out, to scream at the top of your lungs, or to physically destroy the perceived obstacle in your path.
pathB.superpower: Total Mobilization. You have access to a level of raw, explosive power that can move mountains. Your body is ready to protect you at any cost.
pathB.generalGoal: Mechanical cooling. Discharge the heat immediately.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are consumed by internal heat and a pounding heart. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Physiological Sigh. Take two sharp inhales through the nose, followed by one long, slow exhale through the mouth. Repeat 5 times.
body.physioTip: Best for: Rapidly offloading CO2 and activating the vagus nerve to mechanically lower the heart rate in seconds.

mind.text: Panoramic Vision. Stop fixating on one point. Look up and soften your gaze to take in the widest possible view of the room.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'threat-tunnel vision' to peripheral vision, which sends a direct hardware signal to the brainstem to down-regulate.

social.text: The Boundary Declaration. If safe, say out loud: 'I am running too hot to talk. I am stepping away for 5 minutes to reset.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Exercising social agency to prevent a relationship 'collision' while in a high-arousal state.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You are hyper-fixated on the trigger. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Isometric Wall Push. Place both hands flat against a wall and push with 100% of your strength for 10 seconds. Release and breathe.
body.physioTip: Best for: Discharging aggressive motor energy into a neutral, immovable object to simulate a 'fight' completion without destruction.

mind.text: Neutral Scanning. Find and name 5 blue objects in the room. Focus only on the color, not the objects themselves.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the prefrontal cortex back online by engaging in a cold, analytical categorization task.

social.text: The Energy Vent. Ask a safe person: 'I have massive energy right now. Can I just shake it out or vent while you stay near?'
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing co-regulation to anchor the explosive energy without needing to 'fix' the trigger immediately.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are consumed by internal heat and a pounding heart. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Panoramic Vision. Stop fixating on your internal sensations. Look up and soften your gaze to take in the widest possible view of the room's edges.
body.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'internal threat-tunneling' to peripheral vision, which sends a hardware signal to the brainstem to inhibit the sympathetic surge.

mind.text: The Blue Search. Find and name 5 blue objects in the room. Focus purely on the literal color, not what the objects are.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the prefrontal cortex to engage with cold, external data, pulling resources away from the internal 'rage' circuits.

social.text: The Boundary Declaration. If safe, say out loud to the room: 'I am running too hot. I am stepping away for 5 minutes.' Then physically leave the space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Exercising social agency to create physical distance, which tells the brain the 'fight' is being managed through space rather than impact.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside (Fixated)
