body.text: The Isometric Wall Push. Place both hands flat against a wall. Push with 100% of your strength for 10 seconds, feeling the tension in your own arms and legs.
body.physioTip: Best for: Discharging motor energy into the skeleton and a neutral object. This simulates 'fight' completion while pulling awareness back into the muscles.

mind.text: The Physiological Sigh. Take two sharp inhales through the nose, followed by one long, forceful exhale through the mouth. Feel your ribs move.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Mechanically popping the alveoli in the lungs to rapidly offload CO2 and pull the 'emergency brake' on the heart rate from the inside.

social.text: The Energy Vent. If with a safe person, ask: 'I need to shake this out. Can you just watch me for a second?' Then shake your hands and feet vigorously.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'witnessed discharge' to anchor the explosive energy within the safe container of a relationship, pulling the focus back to your own body's release.

pathB.pathB: Exploding and Fixated on the outside,

pathB.title: Flooded
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel flooded. Your sensory inputs are overflowing, and your brain can no longer process the 'data'—it only sees 'danger'. You are not losing control; you are being overwhelmed by your own survival energy.
pathB.blueprint: Sensory Saturation. Your nervous system is receiving more input than your 'processor' can handle, leading to a state of high-arousal confusion and panic.
pathB.physicalSensation: Short, shallow breaths; a feeling of 'pins and needles' or tingling; dizziness; and a sense that the room is spinning or closing in.
pathB.metaphor: A river that has burst its banks during a storm. The water is everywhere, and the current is too fast to stand in.
pathB.theImpulse: To run blindly, to flail, to hyperventilate, and to find an immediate exit at any cost.
pathB.superpower: Hyper-Vigilance. In this state, you are noticing everything. Your system is trying to find a gap in the chaos to lead you toward safety.
pathB.generalGoal: Lowering the water level. Rhythmic anchoring.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are drowning in your own internal sensations. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Rhythmic Stomp. Stomp your feet firmly on the ground. Feel the impact travel through your bones. Left, Right, Left, Right.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using high-intensity bone conduction to provide an undeniable physical anchor when internal sensations feel 'borderless'.

mind.text: 5-4-3-2-1 Sensory Scan. Name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to cycle through different sensory channels, which disrupts the 'panic loop' and re-engages the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: The Bi-Polar Containment. Place one hand firmly on your forehead and the other on your chest. Feel the 'container' of your own touch.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a physical sense of 'closure' and boundary to the upper torso and head during high-arousal flooding.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels loud, chaotic, and 'too much'. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Tunnel Vision Control. Cup your hands around your eyes like goggles to block peripheral vision. Focus only on one small spot in front of you.
body.physioTip: Best for: Reducing the 'visual tax' on the brain by manually limiting incoming data, allowing the system to process one thing at a time.

mind.text: The Sound Anchor. Focus on a single, repetitive sound in the room—a clock ticking, a fan humming, or your own breath.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using a rhythmic, predictable auditory signal to synchronize a racing, chaotic internal tempo.

social.text: Co-Regulated Mirroring. Look at a safe person and try to match their slow, steady breathing pattern.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'mirror neurons' to outsource your regulation to a stable external nervous system.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are drowning in internal panic sensations. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Rhythmic Stomp. Stomp your feet firmly on the ground: Left, Right, Left, Right. Focus entirely on the vibration traveling through your leg bones.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'bone conduction' and high-intensity feedback to provide an undeniable external anchor when internal borders feel lost.

mind.text: 5-4-3-2-1 Sensory Scan. Name 5 things you can see, 4 you can hear, and 3 you can touch in this exact room right now.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the 'Thinking Brain' to cycle through external sensory channels, which disrupts the internal panic loop and re-engages the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: Co-Regulated Mirroring. If a safe person is nearby, look at their shoulders. Try to match the slow, steady rhythm of their movement.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing mirror neurons to 'outsource' your regulation to a stable external nervous system, pulling you out of your internal chaos.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels loud, chaotic, and 'too much'. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Tunnel Vision Control. Cup your hands around your eyes like goggles to block out the room. Focus only on your own hands or a single spot on the floor.
body.physioTip: Best for: Manually reducing the 'visual tax' on the brain by limiting peripheral data, allowing the system to process one internal point at a time.

mind.text: The Internal Sound Anchor. Focus entirely on the sound of your own breath moving in and out of your nose. Count each exhale silently.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using a rhythmic, predictable internal signal to synchronize a racing, chaotic internal tempo.

social.text: Bi-Polar Containment. Place one hand firmly on your forehead and the other on your chest. Squeeze slightly and feel the boundaries of your own body.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a physical sense of 'closure' and boundary to the core, helping a 'spaced out' brain re-inhabit the somatic container.

pathB.title: Twitchy
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel twitchy. You have an enormous amount of 'mobilization' energy ready to go, but nowhere to point it. You are like a racehorse behind the gate.
pathB.blueprint: Unchanneled Sympathetic Energy. Your body has dumped adrenaline into your system to prepare for action, but since you aren't fighting or fleeing, the energy is vibrating in your muscles.
pathB.physicalSensation: Restless legs; bouncing knees; tapping fingers; and a feeling of 'electricity' running under the skin.
pathB.metaphor: A live wire that has fallen on the ground—sparking and jumping with energy that has no grounded destination.
pathB.theImpulse: To pace the room, to bite your nails, to check your phone every 5 seconds, and to move without purpose.
pathB.superpower: Ready-For-Action. You are primed. If a real emergency happened right now, you would be the first person to move.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'buzz' is making you feel anxious internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Burst Discharge. Perform 20 jumping jacks or a quick set of push-ups. Give that 'twitch' a high-intensity job to do.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing a rapid metabolic 'sink' for excess adrenaline, utilizing large muscle groups to burn off the sympathetic surge.

mind.text: Rhythmic Counting. Tap a steady beat on your lap and count out loud: 1-2-3-4, 1-2-3-4.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using the prefrontal cortex to impose a structure on chaotic motor energy, shifting from 'twitch' to 'intentional rhythm'.

social.text: The Percussive Hum. Hum a fast, rhythmic tune with enough volume to feel your chest vibrate. Let it match your internal speed.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using vocal cord vibration to stimulate the auricular branch of the vagus nerve while venting motor energy.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too slow or annoying. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Entrainment Braking. Match your finger tapping to an external rhythm (like a clock), then gradually and consciously slow the tap down over 30 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'entrainment' to manually down-shift the nervous system's internal clock by anchoring it to an external pace.

mind.text: Micro-Sorting. Focus on a single, repetitive manual task: sort coins by size, arrange pens by color, or stack some cards.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Channeling 'jittery' fine motor energy into an analytical, low-stakes task to re-engage the 'Task Positive Network'.

social.text: The GIF Dispatch. Text a friend a high-energy or funny GIF. Use the digital interaction to 'export' the jittery spark.
social.physioTip: Best for: Converting unchanneled physical energy into a brief social 'ping,' reducing the internal pressure of the 'Live Wire'.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'buzz' is making you feel anxious internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Entrainment Braking. Match your finger tapping to an external rhythm (like a clock or a steady beat), then gradually and consciously slow the tap down over 30 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'entrainment' to manually down-shift the nervous system by anchoring it to an external pace, pulling focus out of the internal jitter.

mind.text: Micro-Sorting. Find 10 small objects nearby (coins, pens, paperclips). Sort them by a specific rule: size, color, or weight. Focus entirely on the objects.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Channeling 'jittery' fine motor energy into an analytical external task to re-engage the prefrontal cortex and the environment.

social.text: The GIF Dispatch. Text a safe person a funny or high-energy GIF. Use the digital interaction to 'export' the jittery spark into a social connection.
social.physioTip: Best for: Converting unchanneled physical energy into a brief social 'ping,' which provides an external destination for the 'Live Wire' energy.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too slow or annoying. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Burst Discharge. Perform 15 rapid squats or a quick set of push-ups against the wall. Give your large muscles a high-intensity job to do right now.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing a rapid metabolic 'sink' for excess adrenaline. Large muscle recruitment pulls the 'jitter' back into the skeletal core to be burned off.

mind.text: The Internal Metronome. Tap a steady beat on your own thigh. Count out loud: 1-2-3-4, matching the tap to your voice. Feel the touch on your skin.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using the prefrontal cortex to impose structure on chaotic motor energy, anchoring the brain back into the physical rhythm of the body.

social.text: The Percussive Hum. Hum a low, steady note with enough volume to feel your chest and throat vibrate. Focus entirely on the internal vibration.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using vocal cord vibration to stimulate the vagus nerve while providing an internal sensory anchor to 'ground' the twitchy electricity.

pathB.title: Surging
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel a surge. Something unexpected just happened, and your system has 'spiked' its energy to meet the new information. You are in a state of high-alert appraisal.
pathB.blueprint: Acute Orientation Response. Your system is rapidly shifting gears to assess if a new stimulus is a threat or an opportunity, resulting in a sudden sympathetic surge.
pathB.physicalSensation: A sudden 'gasp' or sharp inhale; wide eyes; a cold chill running down the spine; and a momentary 'freeze' followed by a racing heart.
pathB.metaphor: A power surge in a building during a lightning storm—the lights flicker brightly for a second before the breakers reset.
pathB.theImpulse: To gasp, to stare, to ask 'What was that?', and to feel a sudden prickle of cold or heat.
pathB.superpower: Instant Adaptation. Your system just gave you a massive boost of awareness so you can survive the next 5 seconds, no matter what happens.
pathB.generalGoal: Stabilizing the current. Slowing the return.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The internal 'spike' feels overwhelming. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Resistance Exhale. Inhale for 3 seconds, then exhale through pursed lips (like blowing through a straw) for a count of 8.
body.physioTip: Best for: Creating positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) to mechanically stimulate the vagus nerve and slow the heart rate spike.

mind.text: The Fact Check. Say out loud: 'That was a surprise, and I am safe right now.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using language to override the survival brain's alarm and re-orient the prefrontal cortex to current safety.

social.text: Diaphragm Anchor. Place your hand on your belly and wait until you can feel it move with your breath again.
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-establishing a connection with the 'midline' of the body after a shocking stimulus has 'ejected' focus to the periphery.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside

body.text: The Panoramic Scan. Look slowly to the far left and then slowly to the far right. Take in the full 180-degree view.
body.physioTip: Best for: Activating the neck's proprioceptors and the vestibular system to signal the brain that the environment is clear of immediate threats.

mind.text: Constant Hunt. Identify 3 things in the room that have NOT changed in the last minute (e.g., the floor, the window, the chair).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a 'temporal anchor' to stabilize a brain that feels like it’s been hit by a sudden change.

social.text: The Solid Tether. Reach out and touch a solid, immovable object (a desk, a wall). Notice the temperature and the resistance.
social.physioTip: Best for: Outsourcing your sense of 'solidness' to the physical environment when your internal foundation feels shaky.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside

body.text: The Panoramic Scan. Slowly rotate your head to the far left and then slowly to the far right. Take in the full 180-degree view of the room's corners.
body.physioTip: Best for: Activating cervical proprioceptors and the vestibular system to signal the brainstem that the external environment is currently stable.

mind.text: The Constant Hunt. Identify 3 large things in the room that have NOT changed in the last minute (e.g., the door, the floor, the heavy table).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a 'temporal anchor' to stabilize a brain that feels fragmented by a sudden change, grounding it in external continuity.

social.text: The Fact Check. Say out loud in a clear voice: 'That was a surprise, but the room is still here and I am safe.' Listen to your own voice.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'Thinking Brain' to override the survival brain's alarm, externalizing the logic to re-orient the prefrontal cortex.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels startling or sharp. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Resistance Exhale. Inhale for 3 seconds, then exhale through pursed lips (like blowing through a straw) for a long count of 8. Feel the pressure.
body.physioTip: Best for: Creating positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) to mechanically stimulate the vagus nerve and pull the heart rate back into the body's midline.

mind.text: The Solid Tether. Reach out and place your hand flat against a heavy, immovable object (a wall or desk). Squeeze slightly and feel the resistance in your hand.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using 'closed-chain' tactile input to pull a spaced-out brain back into the physical container of the arm and palm.

social.text: Diaphragm Anchor. Place one hand firmly on your belly and the other on your heart. Wait until you can feel the warmth of your hands on your skin.
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-establishing a connection with the 'Self' after a shock has ejected focus to the periphery, providing a safe, internal embrace.

pathB.title: Burning
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel like you are burning. Your system is radiating high-voltage energy because it's trying to incinerate a perceived threat or injustice. You aren't 'toxic'; you are protective.
pathB.blueprint: Thermal sympathetic activation. Your metabolic rate has spiked to generate heat and power, preparing your body for a high-intensity defense.
pathB.physicalSensation: Internal heat rising toward the face; dry mouth; a prickling sensation in the skin; and eyes that feel sharp or 'stinging'.
pathB.metaphor: A furnace with the door wide open. The heat is useful for forging, but it consumes everything if it's not directed.
pathB.theImpulse: To scorch others with words, to move fast and hard, and to find an immediate outlet for the internal fire.
pathB.superpower: Pure Vitality. You have the energy of a star. When cooled slightly, this is the power of absolute conviction and boundary setting.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The fire is consuming you from within. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Sitali Cooling. Curl your tongue (or purse lips) and inhale slowly as if through a straw. Feel the cold air on your tongue; exhale through the nose.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the moisture on the tongue to create evaporative cooling, which sends an immediate 'temperature drop' signal to the brain.

mind.text: The Blue Mist. Visualize a blue, cool mist entering your lungs with every inhale, neutralizing the heat in your chest.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using color-association imagery to prime the nervous system for down-regulation and thermal settling.

social.text: Thermal Alert. Text a trusted person: 'Running hot. Need a moment of cool silence.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Labeling the internal state to prevent social friction while giving yourself permission to disengage.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's adding fuel to your fire. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Radial Cooling. Run cold water over your wrists for 60 seconds. Focus entirely on the temperature change.
body.physioTip: Best for: Cooling the blood at a high-circulation point (the radial artery) to help lower the core systemic temperature.

mind.text: Thermal Scan. Find one object in the room that looks 'cold' (a metal leg, a glass window) and look at it steadily.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'visual-thermal' cues to shift the brain's focus away from 'hot' emotional triggers.

social.text: The Thermal Pivot. Step away into a cooler room or out into the fresh air to change your immediate 'thermal' environment.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside

body.text: Radial Cooling. Run cold water over your inner wrists for 60 seconds. Focus entirely on the sensation of the cold liquid against your pulse points.
body.physioTip: Best for: Cooling the blood at high-circulation points where the radial artery is superficial, helping to lower systemic core temperature and signal safety.

mind.text: The Cold Search. Find one object in the room that is made of metal, stone, or glass. Look at it and imagine its temperature against your skin.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting the brain from 'hot' emotional triggers to cold environmental data, utilizing visual-thermal association to dampen arousal.

social.text: Thermal Alert. Text a safe person or say out loud: 'Running hot right now. Need a moment of cool silence.' Then move to a cooler physical space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using social agency to change your immediate thermal environment, forcing the body’s sensors to pivot toward homeostatic cooling.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment is adding fuel to your fire. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Sitali Cooling. Curl your tongue into a 'U' shape (or purse your lips) and inhale slowly through the mouth. Feel the cold air hitting the moisture on your tongue.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing evaporative cooling on the tongue to send an immediate 'temperature drop' signal to the brainstem through the glossopharyngeal nerve.

mind.text: The Blue Mist. Close your eyes. Imagine a cool, blue mist entering your lungs with every inhale, neutralizing the 'red' heat in your chest and belly.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visceral imagery to facilitate systemic thermal settling, pulling the focus back to the internal process of regulation.

social.text: Internal Thermal Pivot. Visualize yourself stepping into a cold, quiet forest or a snow-covered field. Feel the silence and the cold on your face.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing a 'safe-place' mental image to simulate an environment that requires a lower metabolic output, pulling you back into a contained state.

pathB.title: Boiling
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you are boiling. You are at the tipping point where liquid energy turns into steam pressure. Your system is ready to erupt to keep you from being suppressed.
pathB.blueprint: High-pressure sympathetic surge. Your blood pressure and heart rate are at peak levels, creating a feeling of internal turbulence.
pathB.physicalSensation: Churning in the stomach; a 'bubbling' feeling in the chest; and a vibrating quality to your limbs and jaw.
pathB.metaphor: A pot on the stove with the lid rattling. The energy is contained, but only just barely.
pathB.theImpulse: To boil over, to snap, to hiss, and to release the pressure through an explosive outburst.
pathB.superpower: Transformative Force. Steam moves engines. This boiling energy is the precursor to massive change if the 'steam' is channeled through a pipe.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The pressure is shaking you from the inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Vagal Toning (VOO). Inhale deeply and make a low, vibrating 'Vooooo' sound on the exhale until your lungs are completely empty.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using sound vibration to stimulate the vagus nerve and provide a mechanical 'vent' for high sympathetic pressure.

mind.text: The Steam Valve. Imagine a pressure valve on top of your head slowly opening, letting out a steady, safe stream of steam.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visualization to facilitate a 'top-down' release of the psychological pressure that fuels the physical surge.

social.text: The Core 'Why'. Say out loud: 'I am furious because [X] mattered to me.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Labeling the values behind the anger to move from 'reactive boiling' to 'principled conviction'.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Everything feels like a trigger. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Weighted Pace. Pace the room with heavy, rhythmic steps. Count each step firmly: 1, 2, 3, 4.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rhythmic bone-loading (stomping) to provide the brain with 'completion' data for the fight-or-flight impulse.

mind.text: Temporal Locking. Look at a clock with a second hand and match your finger-tapping to the steady 'tick'.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Outsourcing your internal 'tempo' to a steady external source to slow down a racing heart and mind.

social.text: Impact Discharge. Ask a safe person for a firm High-Five. Focus on the physical 'slap' and the resistance of their hand.

body.physioTip: Best for: Using rhythmic bone-loading and vocalization to anchor the brain in the external physical environment, pulling awareness away from the internal churn.

mind.text: Temporal Locking. Look at a clock with a second hand (or a digital timer). Match your finger-tapping to the steady 'tick' for 30 seconds.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Outsourcing your internal 'tempo' to a steady external source to manually override a racing, 'boiling' internal pulse.

body.text: Vagal Toning (VOO). Inhale deeply and make a low, vibrating 'Vooooo' sound on the exhale. Focus entirely on the vibration in your chest and throat.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using internal sound vibration to stimulate the vagus nerve branch in the throat, providing a mechanical 'internal vent' for high pressure.

mind.text: The Steam Valve. Close your eyes. Imagine a safe pressure valve on top of your head slowly opening. Visualize the hot steam leaving your body through the valve.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using ideokinesis to facilitate a visceral release of the pressure, pulling the focus back to the internal sensation of cooling.

social.text: Impact Discharge. Press your hands together as hard as possible (the 'prayer' position) and feel the resistance of your own palms.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using a brief, high-intensity isometric impact to discharge motor energy within your own frame, re-establishing your physical boundaries.

pathB.title: Clenched
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you are clenched. Your system has hardened your exterior to protect your vulnerability. You are a fortress under siege.
pathB.blueprint: Protective Armoring. High sympathetic arousal is causing sustained muscular contraction (bracing) to protect your joints and organs from impact.
pathB.physicalSensation: Tight jaw; shoulders up to the ears; fists balled; and a feeling of 'holding on' for dear life.
pathB.metaphor: A fist that has forgotten how to open. Strong, but unable to feel or receive.
pathB.theImpulse: To hold tighter, to refuse help, to grit your teeth, and to muscle through the pain.
pathB.superpower: Unshakeable Strength. In this state, you are an anchor. Nothing can move you or break you while you are braced.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are a prisoner inside your own tension. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Full-Body Flush (PIR). Squeeze every muscle in your body even tighter (fists, jaw, toes) for 5 seconds, then drop everything at once on a long exhale.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing Post-Isometric Relaxation to bypass the brain's 'guarding' reflex and force a systemic muscular release.

mind.text: The Knot Search. Locate the absolute center of the tension in your body. Observe it as a physical object without trying to change it.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'being' the tension to 'observing' the tension, which re-engages the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: The Un-Armoring. Visualize yourself stepping out of a heavy suit of armor in a perfectly safe room, piece by piece.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using symbolic imagery to signal to the nervous system that the 'siege' is over and protection is no longer required.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like a physical threat. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: TMJ Release. Gently massage the hinges of your jaw with your fingertips. Drop your tongue away from the roof of your mouth.
body.physioTip: Best for: Deactivating the 'clench' at the primary cranial trigger point, which helps lower overall sympathetic tone.

mind.text: Pliability Scan. Find and name three objects in the room that are 'soft' or 'flexible' (e.g., a pillow, a plant, a sweater).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Priming the brain with visual cues of 'softness' to counteract the internal 'rigidity' bias.

social.text: Pressure Matching. Ask a safe person for a very firm, steady hand-squeeze. Match their pressure exactly for 5 seconds, then release together.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using co-regulated resistance to satisfy the body's urge to 'hold on' while connecting to a safe external anchor.

social.physioTip: Best for: Deactivating the primary cranial 'clench' trigger point, which helps pull the spaced-out system back into a regulated internal baseline.

pathB.title: Braced
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel braced. You are expecting an impact, so your system is pre-loading your muscles to absorb the blow.
pathB.blueprint: Preparatory Sympathetic Load. Your brain is anticipating a challenge and has 'tightened the strings' of your system to ensure stability.
pathB.physicalSensation: Lower back tension; knees locked; holding the breath; and a feeling of 'readiness' that is starting to ache.
pathB.metaphor: A bridge under a heavy load—straining against the weight, but holding firm.
pathB.theImpulse: To stay in one position, to monitor every sound, and to prepare for the worst.
pathB.superpower: Durability. You are built to endure. Your system is currently maximizing its defensive architecture to keep you upright.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your internal structure is under strain. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Spinal Unload. Place your hands on a table and push down firmly to let your spine grow tall, then slowly soften your elbows and notice if your shoulders drop.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'closed-chain' resistance to re-align the vertebrae and then testing the system's ability to yield as the muscular 'bracing' is removed.

mind.text: The Safe Container. Imagine yourself inside a solid, transparent dome where you are physically untouchable and safe from impact.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a symbolic visual boundary to reduce the 'startle' reflex and the need for constant muscular armor.

social.text: The Safety Ping. Text a trusted person: 'In bracing mode right now. Just sending a safety ping to stay connected.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Outsourcing a sense of safety to a social tether, which can lower the 'guarding' response in the nervous system.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels unpredictable. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Lateral Shift. Shift your weight slowly from your left foot to your right foot. Feel the floor's resistance with every shift.
body.physioTip: Best for: Unlocking the knees and engaging the vestibular system to prove that the ground is stable and supportive.

mind.text: The Horizontal Scan. Find three horizontal lines in the room (shelves, floorboards, window frames). Notice how steady and unmoving they are.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'visual horizon' to stabilize the brain's sense of spatial orientation and reduce high-arousal scanning.

social.text: Wall Surrender. Lean your back fully against a solid wall. Imagine the wall 'taking' the bracing energy from your spine and carrying your weight.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing a large-surface tactile contact to provide the nervous system with undeniable proof of external support.

pathB.title: Racing
pathB.validation: It makes sense that everything feels like it's racing. Your system just had a massive intake of information and is trying to process it at 100mph to catch up.
pathB.blueprint: Tachy-psychia (Time Distortion). High adrenaline is making your brain process the environment faster than normal, making the world seem like it's in fast-forward.
pathB.physicalSensation: Fluttering in the chest; eyes darting; thoughts jumping from topic to topic; and cold sweat.
pathB.metaphor: A film being played at double speed—you can see everything, but you can't feel the meaning of anything.
pathB.theImpulse: To talk fast, to move erratically, and to 'do everything' at once to stop the racing feeling.
pathB.superpower: Rapid Integration. You are a high-speed processor. You can see connections and details that others miss because your 'shutter speed' is so high.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your internal thoughts are a blur. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Parasympathetic Brake (4-7-8). Inhale for 4, hold for 7, exhale through pursed lips for 8. The long exhale is your system's manual brake.
body.physioTip: Best for: Mechanically stimulating the vagus nerve and increasing CO2 levels to rapidly lower the heart rate and calm the brainstem.

mind.text: The Pulse Sync. Find your pulse at your wrist or neck. Count it silently for 30 seconds, focusing only on the steady rhythm.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using interoception to provide a concrete, biological 'metronome' for a racing mind to latch onto.

social.text: Slow-Talk Request. If with a safe person, ask them to talk very slowly about a mundane topic (like a recipe or their commute).
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'social entrainment'—using someone else's slower vocal rhythm to down-regulate your own internal speed.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside

body.text: Honey-Motion Orienting. Rotate your head to look around the room as if you were moving through deep, thick honey—extremely slow.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using slow-motion cervical rotation to 'reset' the vestibular-ocular reflex and reduce visual 'blur'.

mind.text: High-Definition Description. Pick one object and describe its color/texture in extreme detail: 'It is a matte, dusty navy with a slight metallic sheen.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to switch from 'scanning' (fast) to 'inspecting' (slow), clearing the cognitive fog.

social.text: Thermal Grounding. Place your hands flat on a cool, solid surface. Stay there and notice the exact temperature until the racing slows.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using constant thermal and tactile data to 'ground' the excess sympathetic energy into a stable physical object.

pathB.title: Volatile
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel volatile. Your system is highly reactive because it detects a lack of safety. You are like a sensitive alarm system currently set to its highest trigger level.
pathB.blueprint: Sympathetic Hyper-reactivity. Your nervous system is primed for a 'snap' response. The brain is scanning for any further threat to justify an immediate explosion.
pathB.physicalSensation: Hot, shallow breathing; a tight, restless feeling in the chest; and eyes that feel like they are darting or scanning the room rapidly.
pathB.metaphor: Dry brush in a drought. One small spark could start a fire, so your system is bracing for the flame.
pathB.theImpulse: To react sharply to small annoyances, to pace, and to use 'biting' words to create distance.
pathB.superpower: Rapid Response. You are incredibly quick right now. If directed toward a task, you could complete it in record time.
pathB.generalGoal: Lowering the sensitivity. Creating a buffer.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel like you're 'fuming' internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Resistance Exhale. Inhale through your nose and exhale through very small, pursed lips (like a straw). Make the exhale twice as long as the inhale.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using positive expiratory pressure to stimulate the vagus nerve and mechanically override the 'fuming' sympathetic surge.

mind.text: Label the Spark. Identify one specific thing 'fuming' you. Say: '[X] is the spark, but I am the one holding the match.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Creating 'cognitive distance' by labeling the trigger, which shifts the brain from reactive amygdala mode to analytical prefrontal mode.

social.text: The Extinguisher Signal. Text a safe friend a 'fire extinguisher' emoji 🧯. A quick signal that you are 'running hot' and need space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing a social boundary with zero verbal tax, preventing 'explosive' interactions while feeling volatile.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels irritating and abrasive. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Vagal Splash. Splash cool water on your face, eyes, or the back of your neck. Focus entirely on the temperature shift.
body.physioTip: Best for: Triggering the 'Mammalian Dive Reflex' via the trigeminal nerve to rapidly lower heart rate and systemic arousal.

mind.text: The 3-3-3 Grounding. Name 3 things you see, 3 things you hear, and move 3 small parts of your body (fingers, toes, tongue).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to cycle through multiple sensory channels to interrupt the hyper-fixated 'threat scan' of volatility.

social.text: The Sensory Pivot. Physically move to a different room or step outside. Change the lighting or the air temperature.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using a total change in sensory environment to 'reset' the orienting response and lower environmental irritability.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel like you're 'fuming' internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Sensory Pivot. Physically leave the room you are in. Move to a space with different lighting, a different temperature, or step outside.
body.physioTip: Best for: Forcing a total reset of the 'orienting response' by changing the entire data feed, pulling awareness out of the internal 'fume' loop.

mind.text: The 3-3-3 Grounding. Name 3 things you see in the room and 3 distinct sounds you hear. Then move 3 small, separate parts of your body (e.g., big toe, pinky, tongue).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to cycle through multiple external sensory channels, which disrupts the hyper-fixated 'threat scan' typical of volatility.

social.text: The Extinguisher Signal. Text a safe person a 'fire extinguisher' emoji 🧯. If alone, say out loud to the room: 'I am running hot, I need this space to be quiet.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing a social boundary with zero verbal tax, externalizing the need for a 'buffer' before an explosive interaction occurs.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels irritating and abrasive. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Vagal Splash. Splash cool water on your face, focusing on the area around your eyes, or hold a cold compress to the back of your neck. Focus on the temperature shock.
body.physioTip: Best for: Triggering the 'Mammalian Dive Reflex' via the trigeminal nerve to rapidly lower heart rate and pull the system back into a regulated internal baseline.

mind.text: The Resistance Exhale. Inhale through your nose for 3 seconds, then exhale through very small, pursed lips for 6 seconds. Feel the pressure in your chest.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using positive expiratory pressure to stimulate the vagus nerve and mechanically override the sympathetic surge from within the body.

social.text: Label the Spark. Identify the specific thing currently irritating you. Say silently: '[X] is the spark, but I am the one choosing to watch it burn.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Creating 'cognitive distance' by labeling the trigger, which shifts the brain from the reactive amygdala to the analytical prefrontal cortex.

pathB.title: Trembling
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you are trembling. This is your body's natural way of trying to 'shake off' or discharge trapped survival energy. It’s a sign that your system is trying to return to balance.
pathB.blueprint: Neurogenic Tremoring. Your sympathetic system is attempting to release the high-voltage charge of a 'fright' response. It is a biological 'reset' button.
pathB.physicalSensation: Involuntary shivering or shaking in the hands, legs, or jaw; chattering teeth; and a 'fluttering' sensation in the stomach.
pathB.metaphor: A small bird ruffling its feathers after a close call with a predator—shaking to let the fear fall away.
pathB.theImpulse: To hide the shaking, to tighten your muscles to stop the movement, or to feel 'out of control'.
pathB.superpower: The Great Release. Your body is doing the hard work for you. Shaking is the most direct path back to the Green Zone.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The shaking feels scary from the inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Supported Discharge. Lean against a wall and push with your hands. Allow the shaking to continue while focusing on the solid, unmoving support of the wall.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing an external 'unmoving' reference point so the brain feels safe enough to allow the neurogenic tremor to complete its discharge cycle.

mind.text: Frequency Matching (VOO). Make a low, vibrating 'Vooooo' sound. Try to match the vibration of your voice to the tremor in your chest or jaw.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Harmonizing the internal 'fright' vibration with an intentional vocal vibration, which signals the brain that the movement is 'organized' and safe.

social.text: The Calm Witness. Visualize a safe person or pet sitting right next to you, simply watching the shaking with calm, steady, and non-judgmental eyes.
social.physioTip: Best for: Reducing the 'shame' or 'panic' often triggered by involuntary shaking through the internal imagery of safe co-regulation.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel detached from the trembling. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Somatic Containment. Wrap yourself tightly in a blanket or give yourself a very firm 'self-hug' (hands crossing to opposite shoulders).
body.physioTip: Best for: Using deep pressure to define the body's 'envelope,' helping the brain re-tether to the physical self during a high-arousal fright response.

mind.text: Tremor Tracking. Observe the rhythm of the tremors for 10 seconds. Is it fast, slow, or inconsistent? Notice where the epicenter is.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from a state of 'being shaken' to a state of 'observing movement,' which moves the brain from the brainstem back to the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: The Steady Anchor. Ask a safe person to just sit with you and place a steady, unmoving hand firmly on your shoulder or upper back.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using an external human anchor to provide a 'still' point of reference, which helps the nervous system feel supported enough to finish the discharge.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The shaking feels scary from the inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Supported Discharge. Lean your back or hands firmly against a solid wall. Allow the shaking to continue while focusing on the cold, unmoving support of the bricks or wood.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing an external 'immovable' reference point so the brain feels safe enough to allow the neurogenic tremor to complete its discharge cycle without panic.

mind.text: The Calm Witness. Visualize a safe person or pet sitting in the room with you, watching the shaking with steady, kind, and non-judgmental eyes.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from internal 'alarm' to an external 'witness' perspective, reducing the shame-response that often blocks the discharge.

social.text: The Steady Anchor. If someone safe is nearby, ask them to place a steady, unmoving hand firmly on your shoulder. Focus on the stillness of their hand.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using an external human anchor to provide a 'still' point of reference, helping the brain distinguish between the shaking self and the stable world.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel detached or 'spaced out' from the trembling. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Somatic Containment. Wrap yourself tightly in a blanket or give yourself a firm 'self-hug' (hands crossing to opposite shoulders). Squeeze until you feel your ribs.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using deep pressure (DTP) to define the body's 'envelope,' helping the brain re-tether to the physical midline during the tremor.

mind.text: Tremor Tracking. Close your eyes. Observe the rhythm of the tremors for 10 seconds. Is it fast or slow? Locate the 'epicenter'—is it in your jaw, chest, or legs?
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from a state of 'being shaken' to a state of 'observing movement,' which re-engages the prefrontal cortex with the internal body.

social.text: Frequency Matching (VOO). Inhale and make a low, vibrating 'Vooooo' sound on the exhale. Try to match the internal vibration of your voice to the tremor in your body.
social.physioTip: Best for: Harmonizing the 'fright' vibration with an intentional vocal vibration, signaling the brain that the internal movement is organized and intentional.

pathB.title: Pressurized
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel pressurized. You are holding a lot of 'mobilization' fuel inside, but the 'outlets' feel blocked. You are a container of high-potential energy.
pathB.blueprint: Inhibited Sympathetic Drive. Adrenaline is high, but your 'social engagement' system is trying to keep a lid on it, creating an internal pressure cooker effect.
pathB.physicalSensation: Pressure behind the eyes; a tight jaw; and a feeling like your skin is 'too tight' for your body.
pathB.metaphor: A garden hose with a kink in it. The water is trying to flow, but the pressure is building up at the blockage.
pathB.theImpulse: To snap, to grit your teeth, and to find something—anything—to push against.
pathB.superpower: Internal Drive. This pressure is the 'fuel' of change. If you can point it at a task, you will be unstoppable.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The pressure feels like it's building in your head. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Jaw Release. Open your mouth wide as if yawning, then gently massage the masseter muscles (the hinges of your jaw).
body.physioTip: Best for: Deactivating the primary 'bracing' point of the skull to lower the perceived pressure in the head and neck.

mind.text: The Balloon Nozzle. Imagine the pressure is a balloon. With every exhale through your mouth, visualize a steady stream of air escaping the nozzle.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visualization to facilitate a slow, controlled release of sympathetic energy without triggering a crash.

social.text: The Vocal Hiss. Take a deep breath and exhale through your teeth with a long, steady 'Ssssss' sound until empty.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing an auditory and physical 'vent' that mimics the release of a pressure valve, helping to settle the heart rate.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's 'pressing' on you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The 50% Load. Place your hands flat on a wall and push with 50% of your strength. Feel the energy travel from your chest into the wall.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using sub-maximal isometric resistance to 'drain' excess motor energy into an immovable object, creating a sense of boundary.

mind.text: The Horizon Scan. Look as far away as possible—out a window or down a long hallway—to expand your sense of spatial boundaries.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'tunnel vision' of anger by expanding the visual field, which sends a safety signal to the superior colliculus.

social.text: The Boundary Declaration. State out loud: 'I have a lot of energy right now. I am going to take a 5-minute walk to vent.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Exercising social agency to create physical distance before the 'pressure' leads to a reactive snap.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The pressure feels like it's building in your head. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Horizon Scan. Look as far away as possible—out a window or down a long hallway. Follow the furthest horizontal line you can see with your eyes.
body.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'threat-tunnel vision' by expanding the visual field, which signals the superior colliculus to lower the sympathetic 'pressure' signal.

mind.text: The 50% Load. Place your hands flat on a wall or heavy desk. Push with 50% of your strength. Imagine the 'steam' moving from your chest, through your arms, and into the wood/stone.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a sub-maximal 'vent' for motor energy. The external resistance proves you can move the load, which reclaim agency from the 'inside' pressure.

social.text: The Boundary Declaration. State out loud to the room: 'I am running high on energy right now. I need a 5-minute buffer.' Then physically take up more space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Exercising social agency to expand your 'personal space' perimeter, which tells the brain it is no longer being 'squeezed' by external demands.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's 'pressing' on you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Jaw Release. Open your mouth wide in a silent yawn. Use your fingertips to gently massage the hinges of your jaw. Let the tongue drop away from the roof of your mouth.
body.physioTip: Best for: Deactivating the 'Masseter-Temporal' clench. As the primary cranial bracing point, releasing here tells the brain it's safe to inhabit the 'midline' again.

mind.text: The Balloon Nozzle. Close your eyes. Imagine the pressure is a balloon. With every exhale, visualize a steady, narrow stream of air escaping from a nozzle in your chest.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visceral imagery to facilitate a slow, controlled internal discharge of sympathetic energy, preventing a sudden reactive 'snap'.

social.text: The Vocal Hiss. Take a deep inhale through the nose. Exhale through your teeth with a long, steady 'Ssssss' sound. Focus entirely on the vibration in your teeth.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a mechanical and auditory 'vent' that mimics a physical pressure valve. It pulls the spaced-out brain back into the internal rhythm of the breath.

pathB.title: Vibrating
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel like you are vibrating. Your system is 'idling' at a high RPM, ready to move but waiting for a clear direction.
pathB.blueprint: Preparatory Sympathetic Arousal. Your motor neurons are firing at a high frequency to prepare your muscles for action, even if you are standing still.
pathB.physicalSensation: A 'humming' or 'buzzing' feeling in the hands and feet; a butterfly sensation in the stomach; and an inability to sit still.
pathB.metaphor: A beehive in the summer—constant, high-frequency movement that sounds like a single, steady note.
pathB.theImpulse: To fidget, to check your phone repeatedly, and to talk faster than usual.
pathB.superpower: Alert Readiness. You are highly 'online'. Your reaction time is currently at its peak.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'hum' feels anxious internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Butterfly Hug. Cross your arms over your chest and slowly tap your shoulders, alternating left and right, like a slow metronome.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using bilateral alternating stimulation to organize high-frequency sympathetic 'noise' into a coherent, rhythmic signal.

mind.text: The Internal Hum. Hum a steady, low note. Focus on the physical vibration in your throat and chest rather than the sound.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating the laryngeal branch of the vagus nerve to provide an internal 'anchor' for the vibrating sensation.

social.text: The Buzz Ping. Text a friend a 'bee' emoji 🐝. A quick, low-tax acknowledgement of your current 'buzzing' energy.
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing the internal state to reduce the 'social masking' tax, which often adds fuel to nervous vibration.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels a bit blurry or too fast. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: External Entrainment. Find a physical rhythm in the room (clock, fan) and tap your finger to it. Once synchronized, gradually slow your tap.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'motor entrainment' to manually down-shift the nervous system's internal frequency by tethering it to a slower external pace.

mind.text: Texture Labeling. Pick one object and describe its texture in your head using 5 specific sensory words (e.g., grain, grit, cool, weave, slick).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the associative cortex to shift from high-speed 'scanning' to slow-speed 'evaluation,' clearing the nervous blur.

social.text: Prosody Focus. Ask a safe person to tell a boring story. Focus entirely on the melodic 'rise and fall' of their voice rather than the words.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing vocal prosody to signal the social engagement system that the environment is safe, lowering systemic arousal.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'hum' feels anxious internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: External Entrainment. Find a steady physical rhythm in the room (a ticking clock, a humming fan, or a bird chirping). Tap your finger to that external beat.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'motor entrainment' to pull awareness out of the internal 'vibration' and tether the nervous system to a predictable external pace.

mind.text: Texture Labeling. Pick one object in the room. Describe its texture in your head using 5 specific sensory words (e.g., 'gritty', 'cool', 'slick', 'woven', 'dense').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the associative cortex to shift the brain from high-speed 'threat scanning' to slow-speed 'evaluation,' pulling focus into the room.

social.text: The Buzz Ping. Text a safe friend a 'bee' emoji 🐝. If alone, say out loud: 'I am buzzing right now, I need to ground.' Listen to the sound of your voice.
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing the internal state to reduce the 'social masking' tax. Labeling the 'buzz' to the environment tells the brain the secret is out and it can settle.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels a bit blurry or too fast. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Butterfly Hug. Cross your arms over your chest. Slowly tap your shoulders, alternating left and right, like a slow metronome. Feel the touch on your skin.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using bilateral alternating stimulation to pull a 'spaced-out' brain back into its midline and organize sympathetic 'noise' into a coherent signal.

mind.text: The Internal Hum. Hum a steady, low note. Focus entirely on the physical vibration in your throat and chest. Feel the 'hum' moving your own ribs.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating the laryngeal branch of the vagus nerve to provide an internal 'anchor' that matches and then settles the vibrating sensation.

social.text: Prosody Focus. If someone is talking, ignore their words. Focus entirely on the melodic 'rise and fall' of their voice. Feel it as a wave of sound.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing vocal prosody to signal the ventral vagal system that the environment is safe, pulling the 'flickering' focus back into a state of presence.

pathB.title: Fidgety
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel fidgety. You have a surplus of 'mobilization' energy and your system is trying to find a productive way to use it.
pathB.blueprint: Low-level Sympathetic Discharge. Your body is using 'micro-movements' (fidgeting) to bleed off excess adrenaline and keep the system from becoming overloaded.
pathB.physicalSensation: Bouncing legs; tapping fingers; playing with hair or jewelry; and a constant need to shift your posture.
pathB.metaphor: A puppy waiting by the door with a leash in its mouth. Ready to go, eager, and full of undirected movement.
pathB.theImpulse: To start three different tasks at once, to clean something, or to pace while thinking.
pathB.superpower: Active Creativity. This restlessness is the 'itch' that leads to discovery and getting things done.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel an internal 'itch' to move. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Extremity Shake. Shake your hands and feet vigorously for 10 seconds, then stop abruptly and feel the 'tingle' of the blood return.
body.physioTip: Best for: Discharging minor sympathetic build-up by stimulating the distal mechanoreceptors and resetting the muscle spindles.

mind.text: The 2-Minute Triage. Identify 3 tiny tasks you can do in under 2 minutes (e.g., water a plant, clear a dish) and do ONE now.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Channeling restless motor energy into a 'completion loop,' which provides a small dopamine hit and settles the system.

social.text: The Micro-Service. Complete one tiny physical task that benefits your environment or someone else (e.g., tidying a shared space).
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'tend-and-befriend' circuit to shift away from jittery sympathetic arousal toward social regulation.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Everything feels like a distraction. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Object Manipulation. Find a 'fidget' object—a pen to click, a stone to rub, or fabric to fold. Focus on the resistance of the object.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing a continuous sensory-motor loop that occupies the 'excess' motor drive, allowing the brain to focus on other tasks.

mind.text: The Texture Map. Focus on the feeling of your clothes against your skin as you move. Is the texture rough, smooth, or heavy?
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from outward-focused distraction to inward-focused tactile grounding to 'anchor' the restlessness.

social.text: The Resistance Game. If with someone, engage in a quick thumb-war or a brief 'palm-push' match. Feel the counter-pressure.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using high-contrast physical resistance with another person to rapidly discharge restless energy through co-regulation.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel an internal 'itch' to move. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Resistance Game. If with someone, engage in a quick thumb-war or a brief 'palm-push' match. If alone, press your palms together firmly for 10 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using high-contrast physical resistance to provide an immediate external anchor, discharging restless energy through interaction with the environment.

mind.text: The 2-Minute Triage. Identify 3 tiny physical tasks you can see in the room (e.g., move a glass, straighten a pillow, close a drawer). Do ONE right now.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Channeling restless motor energy into a 'completion loop,' which provides an external dopamine hit and anchors the brain in the room.

social.text: The Micro-Service. Complete one tiny physical task that benefits the space or someone else (e.g., tidying a shared surface or refilling a water bottle).
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'tend-and-befriend' circuit to shift away from internal jittery arousal toward external social regulation.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Everything feels like a distraction. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Extremity Shake. Shake your hands and feet vigorously for 10 seconds. Stop abruptly and focus entirely on the 'tingle' of the blood returning to your fingers.
body.physioTip: Best for: Discharging sympathetic build-up and then immediately pulling focus back to the skin's surface and muscle spindles to re-occupy the body.

mind.text: The Texture Map. Close your eyes. Focus on the sensation of your clothes against your skin. Move slightly and identify if the texture is rough, smooth, or heavy.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from outward-focused distraction to inward-focused tactile grounding, pulling the 'spaced-out' brain back into the somatic envelope.

social.text: Object Manipulation. Find a 'fidget' object—a pen to click, a stone to rub, or a piece of fabric. Focus entirely on the internal resistance of the object against your fingers.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a continuous sensory-motor loop that occupies the 'excess' motor drive, anchoring the self back to the hands.

pathB.title: Pounding
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel a pounding sensation. Your heart is increasing its tempo to prepare your muscles for action. Even if you aren't moving, your body is ready to protect you.
pathB.blueprint: Cardiovascular Activation. Your sympathetic nervous system has increased your heart rate and stroke volume, making the pulse feel more prominent in your chest, neck, or ears.
pathB.physicalSensation: A rhythmic thumping in the chest; a pulsing feeling in the temples or throat; and a sense of internal 'drums' beating faster than the environment.
pathB.metaphor: An engine idling at a high speed, ready for the race to start. The vibration is felt through the whole frame of the car.
pathB.theImpulse: To check your pulse, to breathe rapidly, and to look for an immediate way to 'quiet' the noise in your chest.
pathB.superpower: Circulatory Power. Your body is efficiently moving oxygen and fuel exactly where it needs to go. You are physically primed for high performance.
pathB.generalGoal: Steadying the rhythm. Externalizing the beat.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The internal 'thump' is making you feel more anxious. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Baroreceptor Reset. Inhale for 4 seconds, then exhale through pursed lips (like a straw) for a full 8 seconds. Repeat 5 times.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using PEEP (Positive End-Expiratory Pressure) to stimulate the baroreceptors and the vagus nerve, mechanically forcing a decrease in heart rate.

mind.text: Serial 7s. Count backward from 100 by subtracting 7 each time (100, 93, 86...). Focus entirely on the math.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to exit the 'limbic hijack' by engaging the prefrontal cortex in a high-load analytical task.

social.text: The Heart Acknowledgment. Place a firm hand over your heart. Say out loud: 'I hear you, heart. Thank you for the energy. We are safe.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'self-compassion' cues and vocalization to reduce the fear-response to internal sensations (interoceptive exposure).

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels too loud or fast for your heartbeat. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Rhythmic Entrainment. Tap a slow, steady beat on your thigh. Focus on the sensation of your hand hitting your leg, creating a new, slower rhythm.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'motor-pacing' to provide the brain with a competing, intentional rhythm that overrides the involuntary pounding of the heart.

mind.text: Square Tracing. Find 5 square or rectangular objects in the room and slowly trace their four corners with your eyes.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the ocular muscles in a structured, geometric pattern to settle the visual field and reduce scanning-related anxiety.

social.text: Prosody Anchor. If with a safe person, focus entirely on the low, steady 'hum' or rhythm of their voice rather than their words.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing external co-regulation to synchronize your internal tempo with a stable social signal.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The internal 'thump' is making you feel more anxious. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Square Tracing. Find 5 rectangular objects in the room (a window, a book, a screen). Slowly trace their four corners with your eyes, counting 1-2-3-4 for each.
body.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the oculomotor muscles in a structured geometric pattern to 'mark' the external environment, pulling focus away from internal cardiac noise.

mind.text: Serial 7s. Count backward from 100 by subtracting 7 each time (100, 93, 86...). Focus entirely on the math and say the numbers out loud.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to exit a 'limbic hijack' by engaging the prefrontal cortex in a high-load analytical task, overriding internal panic signals.

social.text: Prosody Anchor. If someone is speaking, ignore their words. Focus entirely on the melodic 'rise and fall' of their voice. Use it to tether yourself to the room.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing external co-regulation to synchronize your internal tempo with a stable social signal, anchoring you in the shared space.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels too loud or fast for your heartbeat. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Baroreceptor Reset. Inhale through the nose for 4 seconds, then exhale through pursed lips (like a straw) for a slow 8 seconds. Feel the pressure change in your chest.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using PEEP (Positive End-Expiratory Pressure) to stimulate the baroreceptors and the vagus nerve, mechanically forcing the heart rate to drop from within.

mind.text: Rhythmic Entrainment. Tap a slow, steady beat on your own thigh. Focus on the physical contact of your hand against your leg. Make the tap the dominant rhythm.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using 'motor-pacing' to provide the brain with a competing, intentional tactile rhythm that overrides the involuntary pounding of the pulse.

social.text: The Heart Acknowledgment. Place your own hand firmly over your heart. Say silently: 'I hear you, heart. Thank you for the energy. We are inside, we are safe.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'self-affiliation' and tactile containment to reduce the fear-response to internal sensations, pulling awareness back to the somatic midline.

pathB.title: Guarded
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel guarded. Your system is protecting your softest parts because it's not yet sure if the current environment is fully safe.
pathB.blueprint: Defensive Armoring. A state of mild sympathetic arousal where the muscles of the core, jaw, and shoulders slightly contract to protect vital areas from potential impact.
pathB.physicalSensation: Shoulders slightly raised; jaw held tight; and a subtle 'bracing' in the belly. You feel like you are wearing an invisible suit of armor.
pathB.metaphor: A drawbridge that has been raised halfway. You aren't closed off, but you are carefully controlling who and what gets in.
pathB.theImpulse: To cross your arms, to weigh your words carefully, and to monitor the movements of others in the room.
pathB.superpower: Strategic Discernment. You are not being 'cold'; you are being wise. You are curating your vulnerability until safety is proven.
pathB.generalGoal: Testing the perimeter. Softening the edges.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel tight and 'walled in' internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Lateral Costal Breathing. Place your hands on the sides of your ribcage. Inhale and try to push your hands out to the sides with your ribs.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rib expansion to create internal space without the 'vulnerability' of a full belly breath, softening the intercostal tension.

mind.text: The Safe Haven. Imagine a specific physical place where you feel 100% safe. Visualize the colors and temperature of that place.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using mental architecture to provide the safety data that the current environment is failing to deliver.

social.text: The Protective Rub. Gently rub your own upper arms or shoulders. Focus on the warmth and the intentionality of the touch.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing 'self-soothing' tactile input to lower the guarding reflex in the trapezius and shoulders.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's watching you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The 180-Degree Scan. Slowly turn your head to the far left and far right. Notice 3 neutral objects that are definitively 'not a threat'.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using cervical rotation to signal the superior colliculus that the periphery is safe, allowing the 'braced' posture to settle.

mind.text: Detail Appreciation. Find one object you find interesting. Silently list 3 precise details (e.g., 'the grain of the wood', 'the shadow on the wall').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'threat-scanning' (broad and fast) to 'detailed observation' (narrow and slow), which settles the amygdala.

social.text: The Half-Smile (Vagal Ping). Look at a neutral object and give it a tiny, almost invisible 'half-smile'. Notice how your jaw and eyes feel.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the facial feedback loop to trigger the ventral vagal system and 'un-clench' the social engagement muscles.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel tight and 'walled in' internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The 180-Degree Scan. Slowly turn your head to the far left and far right. Notice 3 neutral objects (e.g., a door handle, a chair leg) that are definitively unmoving.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using cervical rotation to signal the superior colliculus that the periphery is clear, allowing the brain to release 'braced' postural muscles.

mind.text: Detail Appreciation. Pick one interesting object in the room. Silently list 3 precise details (e.g., 'the grain of the wood', 'the way light hits the edge').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'broad threat-scanning' to 'narrow detailed observation,' which forces the amygdala to yield to the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: The Half-Smile (Vagal Ping). Look at a neutral object and give it a tiny, almost invisible 'half-smile'. Notice the slight release in your jaw and around your eyes.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the facial feedback loop to trigger the ventral vagus, signaling the brain that the environment is safe enough for social engagement.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's watching you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Lateral Costal Breathing. Place your hands on the sides of your ribcage. Inhale and try to push your hands out to the sides with your ribs. Feel the expansion.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rib expansion to create internal space without the 'vulnerability' of a full belly breath, softening the intercostal tension from the inside out.

mind.text: The Safe Haven. Close your eyes for 5 seconds. Imagine a specific place where you feel 100% safe. Visualize the colors, the ground beneath you, and the temperature.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using mental architecture to provide the 'safety data' that the current environment is failing to deliver, pulling awareness back to a centered self.

social.text: The Protective Rub. Gently rub your own upper arms or shoulders with your palms. Focus entirely on the warmth and the texture of your own skin.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing 'self-soothing' tactile input to lower the guarding reflex in the trapezius, re-establishing your body as a safe place to be.

pathB.title: Scanning
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you are scanning. Your brain is trying to predict and prevent problems by looking for any small cue of trouble. Your 'radar' is just working overtime.
pathB.blueprint: Hyper-Vigilant Orienting. The nervous system has narrowed your visual and auditory focus to detect threats, often leading to 'tunnel vision' or an inability to rest the eyes.
pathB.physicalSensation: Eyes darting from object to object; a feeling of 'lightness' in the head; and a neck that feels slightly stiff from constant movement.
pathB.metaphor: A lighthouse beam swinging rapidly across the water, searching for a rock that might not even be there.
pathB.theImpulse: To check your phone, to look at the door, and to mentally rehearse 'what-if' scenarios over and over.
pathB.superpower: High-Speed Pattern Recognition. You are currently a genius at noticing details. Your system is protecting your future by analyzing the present.
pathB.generalGoal: Grounding the gaze. Broadening the view.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your thoughts are scanning for 'what-ifs'. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Hallux Grounding. Press your big toes (hallux) firmly into the ground. Feel the solid, undeniable fact of the floor.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using a distal, high-contrast pressure point to pull awareness away from the 'head-heavy' anxiety of scanning.

mind.text: The Evidence Check. Take one specific 'what-if' and ask: 'Is there physical proof for this in this room right now?'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'predictive coding' loop of the brain by demanding real-time sensory data over future-based simulations.

social.text: The Verbal Anchor. Say out loud: 'I am here, and I am currently [state your physical action, e.g., sitting in a chair].'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using your own voice to create an 'auditory boundary' that anchors you in the present moment.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Your eyes can't stop moving around the room. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Panoramic Vision. Without moving your head, soften your gaze to see the far-left and far-right edges of the room at the same time.
body.physioTip: Best for: Switching from foveal vision (sympathetic) to peripheral vision (parasympathetic), which inhibits the startle response.

mind.text: Targeted Categorization. Find 3 green things, 3 red things, and 3 yellow things. Say their names silently.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Giving the 'scanning' reflex an analytical job to do, which re-engages the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: Neutral Fixation. Pick a neutral, non-human object (a door handle or light switch) and look at it steadily for 10 seconds.
social.physioTip: Best for: Practicing 'visual stillness' to prove to the nervous system that you can stop the threat-scan without being harmed.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your thoughts are scanning for 'what-ifs'. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Panoramic Vision. Soften your gaze until you can see the far-left and far-right edges of the room at the same time without moving your head.
body.physioTip: Best for: Switching from foveal vision (sympathetic) to peripheral vision (parasympathetic), which sends a hardware signal to the brainstem to inhibit the 'threat-scan' and startle response.

mind.text: Targeted Categorization. Find 3 green things, 3 red things, and 3 yellow things in the room. Say their names silently as you find them.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Giving the 'scanning' reflex a specific, non-threatening analytical job to do, which re-engages the prefrontal cortex and anchors awareness in the room.

social.text: Neutral Fixation. Pick one non-human, neutral object (like a light switch or door handle). Look at it steadily for 10 seconds without letting your eyes jump.
social.physioTip: Best for: Practicing 'visual stillness' to prove to the nervous system that you can stop the environmental scan safely, breaking the worry-loop.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Your eyes can't stop moving around the room. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Hallux Grounding. Press your big toes (hallux) firmly into the floor. Feel the solid, undeniable resistance of the ground beneath your feet.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using a distal, high-contrast pressure point to pull a 'light-headed' or spaced-out brain back down into the somatic container.

mind.text: The Evidence Check. Take one specific 'what-if' and ask: 'Is there physical, sensory proof for this in my body or this room right now?'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'predictive coding' loop by demanding real-time interoceptive data over future-based simulations, pulling focus back to the self.

social.text: The Verbal Anchor. Say out loud in a clear whisper: 'I am here, and I am currently [state your action, e.g., sitting]'. Feel the vibration in your throat.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using your own voice to create an 'auditory boundary' that pulls a dissociated consciousness back into the midline.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathB.title: Flushed
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel flushed. Your system is sending blood to your skin and face as part of a temperature-regulating and emotional response. It’s a visible sign of your internal heat.
pathB.blueprint: Vasodilation. Sympathetic arousal causes the blood vessels near the surface of the skin to widen, often as a reaction to social stress or rising anger.
pathB.physicalSensation: Heat in the cheeks, ears, or chest; a 'tingly' or 'prickly' feeling in the face; and a sense that the room has suddenly become warmer.
pathB.metaphor: A boiler that has been turned up just one notch too high—not exploding, but the pipes are hot to the touch.
pathB.theImpulse: To fan yourself, to look away, to splash water on your face, and to hide the visible 'redness'.
pathB.superpower: Honest Expression. Your body is telling the truth. Being flushed is a powerful, non-verbal signal that a boundary has been touched.
pathB.generalGoal: Cooling the current. Soothing the surface.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the heat rising within. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Radial Sink. Run cold water over your inner wrists for 30 seconds. Focus entirely on the temperature shift in your pulse points.
body.physioTip: Best for: Cooling the blood at a high-circulation point (radial artery) to help pull the systemic thermal energy out of the core and face.

mind.text: The Horizon Anchor. Look out a window at the furthest point possible. Notice the vast amount of space between your eyes and the horizon.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Expanding the visual field to break the 'social-exposure' tunnel vision, signaling the hypothalamus to settle the systemic heat.

social.text: The Buffer Step. Physically move your body back or away by 2 feet. Acknowledge the extra 'personal space' you have just created around you.
social.physioTip: Best for: Adjusting the personal-space perimeter to reduce the 'proximity alert' in the brain that often maintains a facial flush.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too 'close' or irritating. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Sitali Cooling. Curl your tongue (or purse lips) and inhale slowly through the opening. Feel the cold air on your tongue; exhale through the nose.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing evaporative cooling on the tongue to send an immediate internal 'temperature drop' signal to the brainstem and hypothalamus.

mind.text: Blue Submersion. Close your eyes. Visualize a cool, calm pool of deep blue water. Imagine the heat leaving your face and chest as you dip your core into it.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using color-thermal association to prime the nervous system for vasoconstriction, pulling awareness back to a centered, cool self.

social.text: Thermal Conductance. Place the backs of your hands (usually the coolest part of your skin) firmly against your warm cheeks. Feel the heat transfer into your palms.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing an immediate, self-regulated 'heat sink' to inhabit your boundaries and reduce the feeling of social exposure.

pathB.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

pathB.title: Prickly
pathB.validation: It makes sense that you feel prickly. Your system is raising its 'quills' to keep people at a safe distance while you navigate a minor annoyance.
pathB.blueprint: Mild Defensive Reactivity. A state where the threshold for 'irritation' is lowered, making neutral sounds or comments feel like sharp pokes.
pathB.physicalSensation: A 'zinging' feeling in the skin; an urge to itch or twitch; and a general feeling of being 'on edge' or easily poked.
pathB.metaphor: A cactus in a busy room. You are well-protected, but anyone who gets too close might get a little sting.
pathB.theImpulse: To make a sharp comment, to roll your eyes, to move away from touch, and to 'bristle' at questions.
pathB.superpower: Sensory Sensitivity. You are noticing exactly what is not working in the environment. This is the first step toward refinement and change.
pathB.generalGoal: Smoothing the quills. Softening the contact.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
